Before reading downloaded images with the Read tool, verify that the file is a valid image rather than HTML by using a CLI tool such as the 'file' command. When corrupted or non-image files, such as GitHub \"Not Found\" pages saved as `.png`, are read, they can cause \"Could not process image\" errors and crash the AI solver process. When the file command shows \"HTML\", \"text\", or \"ASCII text\", the download failed, so do not call Read on that file. Instead: (1) when images are from GitHub issues or PRs, such as URLs containing \"github.com/user-attachments\", retry with: curl -L -H \"Authorization: token \$(gh auth token)\" -o <filename> \"<url>\" (2) when the retry still fails, skip the image and note that it was unavailable.

[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When you face something extremely hard, use divide and conquer.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO] 
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO] Initial research.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When you start, create a detailed plan for yourself and follow your todo list step by step. Add as many relevant points from these guidelines to the todo list as practical so you can track the work clearly.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When the user mentions CI failures or asks to investigate logs, consider adding these todos to track the investigation: (1) list recent CI runs with timestamps, (2) download logs from failed runs to the ci-logs/ directory, (3) analyze error messages and identify the root cause, (4) implement a fix, (5) verify that the fix resolves the specific errors found in the logs.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When you read the issue, read all details and comments thoroughly.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When you see screenshots or images in issue descriptions, pull request descriptions, comments, or discussions, download the image to a local file first, then use the Read tool to view and analyze it. Before reading downloaded images with the Read tool, verify that the file is a valid image rather than HTML by using a CLI tool such as the 'file' command. When corrupted or non-image files, such as GitHub "Not Found" pages saved as `.png`, are read, they can cause "Could not process image" errors and crash the AI solver process. When the file command shows "HTML", "text", or "ASCII text", the download failed, so do not call Read on that file. Instead: (1) when images are from GitHub issues or PRs, such as URLs containing "github.com/user-attachments", retry with: curl -L -H "Authorization: token $(gh auth token)" -o <filename> "<url>" (2) when the retry still fails, skip the image and note that it was unavailable.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When you need issue details, use gh issue view https://github.com/link-assistant/model-in-browser/issues/11.

[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When you need repo context, read files in your working directory.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When you study related work, study the most recent related pull requests.

[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO] Solution development and testing.

[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When implementing features, search for similar existing implementations in the codebase and use them as examples instead of implementing everything from scratch.

[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]       start from testing of small functions using separate scripts;
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]       write unit tests with mocks for easy and quick start.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When you test integrations, use existing framework.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When you test solution draft, include automated checks in pr.

[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When you see repeated test timeout patterns in CI, investigate the root cause rather than increasing timeouts.

[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When you need human help, use gh pr comment 12 --body "your message" to comment on existing PR.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO] 
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO] Reproducible testing.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When fixing a bug, create a test that reproduces the problem before implementing the fix. When you cannot reproduce the problem, you cannot verify the fix.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When encountering logic bugs, write an automated test that fails due to the bug, then implement the fix to make it pass.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When encountering UI bugs, capture a screenshot showing the problem state, then create a visual regression test or manual verification screenshot after the fix.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When creating tests, prefer minimum reproducible examples, meaning the simplest test case that demonstrates the issue.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When submitting a fix, include in the PR description: (1) how to reproduce the issue, (2) the automated test that verifies the fix, (3) before/after screenshots for UI issues.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When a bug fix does not have a reproducing test, treat the fix as incomplete because regressions can occur later without notice.

[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO] GitHub CLI command patterns.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When fetching lists from GitHub API, use the --paginate flag to ensure all results are returned (GitHub returns max 30 per page by default).
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When listing PR review comments (inline code comments), use gh api repos/OWNER/REPO/pulls/NUMBER/comments --paginate.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When listing PR conversation comments, use gh api repos/OWNER/REPO/issues/NUMBER/comments --paginate.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When listing PR reviews, use gh api repos/OWNER/REPO/pulls/NUMBER/reviews --paginate.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When listing issue comments, use gh api repos/OWNER/REPO/issues/NUMBER/comments --paginate.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When adding PR comment, use gh pr comment NUMBER --body "text" --repo OWNER/REPO.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When adding issue comment, use gh issue comment NUMBER --body "text" --repo OWNER/REPO.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When viewing PR details, use gh pr view NUMBER --repo OWNER/REPO.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When filtering with jq, use gh api repos/${owner}/${repo}/pulls/${prNumber}/comments --paginate --jq 'reverse | .[0:5]'.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO] 
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO] Playwright MCP usage (browser automation via mcp__playwright__* tools).
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When you develop frontend web applications (HTML, CSS, JavaScript, React, Vue, Angular, etc.), use Playwright MCP tools to test the UI in a real browser.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When WebFetch tool fails to retrieve expected content (e.g., returns empty content, JavaScript-rendered pages, or login-protected pages), use Playwright MCP tools (browser_navigate, browser_snapshot) as a fallback for web browsing.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When WebSearch tool fails or returns insufficient results, use Playwright MCP tools (browser_navigate, browser_snapshot) as a fallback for internet search.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When you need to interact with dynamic web pages that require JavaScript execution, use Playwright MCP tools.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When you need to visually verify how a web page looks or take screenshots, use browser_take_screenshot from Playwright MCP.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When you need to fill forms, click buttons, or perform user interactions on web pages, use Playwright MCP tools (browser_click, browser_type, browser_fill_form).
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When you need to test responsive design or different viewport sizes, use browser_resize from Playwright MCP.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When you finish using the browser, close it with browser_close to free resources.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When reproducing UI bugs, use browser_take_screenshot to capture the problem state before implementing any fix.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When fixing UI bugs, take before/after screenshots to provide visual evidence of the fix for human verification.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When creating UI tests, save baseline screenshots to the repository for visual regression testing.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When verifying UI fixes, compare screenshots to ensure the fix does not introduce unintended visual changes.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]

[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When uploading images, commit them to the branch first, then reference them using the GitHub blob URL format with ?raw=true suffix (works for both public and private repositories).
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When the visual result is important for review, mention it explicitly in the pull request description with the embedded image.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When fixing UI bugs, capture both the "before" (problem) and "after" (fixed) screenshots as evidence for human verification.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When reporting UI bugs, include a screenshot of the problem state to enable visual verification of the fix.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When the fix is visual, include side-by-side or sequential comparison of before/after states in the PR description.
[2026-06-09T22:47:52.508Z] [INFO]    - When possible, create automated visual regression tests to prevent the UI bug from recurring.

no server processing required!\n\n[![CI/CD Pipeline](https://github.com/link-assistant/model-in-browser/workflows/CI%2FCD%20Pipeline/badge.svg)](https://github.com/link-assistant/model-in-browser/actions)\n[![Deploy to GitHub Pages](https://github.com/link-assistant/model-in-browser/workflows/Build%20and%20Deploy%20to%20GitHub%20Pages/badge.svg)](https://github.com/link-assistant/model-in-browser/actions)\n[![License: Unlicense](https://img.shields.io/badge/license-Unlicense-blue.svg)](http://unlicense.org/)\n\n## Features\n\n- **100% Client-Side**: All AI inference happens in your browser - no data sent to servers\n- **WebAssembly Powered**: Rust compiled to WASM for near-native performance\n- **Web Worker**: Model runs in background thread for responsive UI\n- **React Chat UI**: Modern chat interface using [@chatscope/chat-ui-kit-react](https://github.com/chatscope/chat-ui-kit-react)\n- **SmolLM2-135M**: Compact 135M parameter model optimized for edge deployment\n- **GitHub Pages Ready**: Deploy as a static site with no backend required\n\n## Demo\n\nVisit the [live demo](https://link-assistant.github.io/model-in-browser/) to try the model in your browser.\n\n> **Note**: First load downloads ~270MB of model weights. The model is cached by your browser for subsequent visits.\n\n## Architecture\n\n```\n┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐\n│                        Browser                               │\n│  ┌─────────────────┐    ┌─────────────────────────────────┐ │\n│  │   React Chat    │    │        Web Worker                │ │\n│  │   UI (Main      │◄──►│  ┌─────────────────────────┐    │ │\n│  │   Thread)       │    │  │   WASM Module           │    │ │\n│  │                 │    │  │  ┌─────────────────┐    │    │ │\n│  │  @chatscope/    │    │  │  │  Candle (Rust)  │    │    │ │\n│  │  chat-ui-kit    │    │  │  │  SmolLM2-135M   │    │    │ │\n│  └─────────────────┘    │  │  └─────────────────┘    │    │ │\n│                         │  └─────────────────────────┘    │ │\n│                         └─────────────────────────────────┘ │\n└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘\n```\n\n## Quick Start\n\n### Prerequisites\n\n- [Rust](https://rustup.rs/) (1.70+)\n- [wasm-pack](https://rustwasm.github.io/wasm-pack/installer/)\n- [Node.js](https://nodejs.org/) (18+)\n\n### Development Setup\n\n```bash\n# Clone the repository\ngit clone https://github.com/link-assistant/model-in-browser.git\ncd model-in-browser\n\n# Build the WASM package\n./scripts/build-wasm.sh\n\n# Install web dependencies and start dev server\ncd web\nnpm install\nnpm run dev\n```\n\nOpen http://localhost:5173 in your browser.\n\n### Build for Production\n\n```bash\n# Build WASM\ncd wasm && wasm-pack build --target web --out-dir ../web/src/pkg\n\n# Build web app\ncd web && npm run build\n\n# Serve with the Rust server\ncargo run --manifest-path server/Cargo.toml -- --dir web/dist\n```\n\n## Project Structure\n\n```\n.\n├── wasm/                    # Rust WASM library for model inference\n│   ├── src/lib.rs           # SmolLM2 WASM bindings\n│   └── Cargo.toml           # WASM package config\n├── web/                     # React web application\n│   ├── src/\n│   │   ├── App.tsx          # Main chat component\n│   │   ├── worker.ts        # Web Worker for inference\n│   │   └── pkg/             # Built WASM package\n│   ├── package.json\n│   └── vite.config.ts\n├── server/                  # Local development server\n│   └── src/main.rs          # Axum server with CORS\n├── .github/workflows/\n│   ├── release.yml          # CI/CD pipeline\n│   └── deploy.yml           # GitHub Pages deployment\n└── scripts/",

no server processing required!\n\n[![CI/CD Pipeline](https://github.com/link-assistant/model-in-browser/workflows/CI%2FCD%20Pipeline/badge.svg)](https://github.com/link-assistant/model-in-browser/actions)\n[![Deploy to GitHub Pages](https://github.com/link-assistant/model-in-browser/workflows/Build%20and%20Deploy%20to%20GitHub%20Pages/badge.svg)](https://github.com/link-assistant/model-in-browser/actions)\n[![License: Unlicense](https://img.shields.io/badge/license-Unlicense-blue.svg)](http://unlicense.org/)\n\n## Features\n\n- **100% Client-Side**: All AI inference happens in your browser - no data sent to servers\n- **WebAssembly Powered**: Rust compiled to WASM for near-native performance\n- **Web Worker**: Model runs in background thread for responsive UI\n- **React Chat UI**: Modern chat interface using [@chatscope/chat-ui-kit-react](https://github.com/chatscope/chat-ui-kit-react)\n- **SmolLM2-135M**: Compact 135M parameter model optimized for edge deployment\n- **GitHub Pages Ready**: Deploy as a static site with no backend required\n\n## Demo\n\nVisit the [live demo](https://link-assistant.github.io/model-in-browser/) to try the model in your browser.\n\n> **Note**: First load downloads ~270MB of model weights. The model is cached by your browser for subsequent visits.\n\n## Architecture\n\n```\n┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐\n│                        Browser                               │\n│  ┌─────────────────┐    ┌─────────────────────────────────┐ │\n│  │   React Chat    │    │        Web Worker                │ │\n│  │   UI (Main      │◄──►│  ┌─────────────────────────┐    │ │\n│  │   Thread)       │    │  │   WASM Module           │    │ │\n│  │                 │    │  │  ┌─────────────────┐    │    │ │\n│  │  @chatscope/    │    │  │  │  Candle (Rust)  │    │    │ │\n│  │  chat-ui-kit    │    │  │  │  SmolLM2-135M   │    │    │ │\n│  └─────────────────┘    │  │  └─────────────────┘    │    │ │\n│                         │  └─────────────────────────┘    │ │\n│                         └─────────────────────────────────┘ │\n└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘\n```\n\n## Quick Start\n\n### Prerequisites\n\n- [Rust](https://rustup.rs/) (1.70+)\n- [wasm-pack](https://rustwasm.github.io/wasm-pack/installer/)\n- [Node.js](https://nodejs.org/) (18+)\n\n### Development Setup\n\n```bash\n# Clone the repository\ngit clone https://github.com/link-assistant/model-in-browser.git\ncd model-in-browser\n\n# Build the WASM package\n./scripts/build-wasm.sh\n\n# Install web dependencies and start dev server\ncd web\nnpm install\nnpm run dev\n```\n\nOpen http://localhost:5173 in your browser.\n\n### Build for Production\n\n```bash\n# Build WASM\ncd wasm && wasm-pack build --target web --out-dir ../web/src/pkg\n\n# Build web app\ncd web && npm run build\n\n# Serve with the Rust server\ncargo run --manifest-path server/Cargo.toml -- --dir web/dist\n```\n\n## Project Structure\n\n```\n.\n├── wasm/                    # Rust WASM library for model inference\n│   ├── src/lib.rs           # SmolLM2 WASM bindings\n│   └── Cargo.toml           # WASM package config\n├── web/                     # React web application\n│   ├── src/\n│   │   ├── App.tsx          # Main chat component\n│   │   ├── worker.ts        # Web Worker for inference\n│   │   └── pkg/             # Built WASM package\n│   ├── package.json\n│   └── vite.config.ts\n├── server/                  # Local development server\n│   └── src/main.rs          # Axum server with CORS\n├── .github/workflows/\n│   ├── release.yml          # CI/CD pipeline\n│   └── deploy.yml           # GitHub Pages deployment\n└── scripts/",

library\n- **React 18** with TypeScript\n- **Vite** as build tool\n- **Custom CSS** with dark theme overrides\n\n### Current UI Features\n- Dark theme with custom CSS overrides for chatscope components\n- Status indicator (loading/ready/error)\n- Progress bar for model download\n- Typing indicator\n- Message input with send button\n- Streaming token display\n\n### Areas for Improvement (as identified in the issue)\n- Need for more modern styling\n- More minimalistic design\n- Better mobile responsiveness\n- Markdown support for formatted messages\n\n---\n\n## React Chat UI Libraries [2026-06-09T22:48:17.728Z] [INFO]         "content": "=== wasm/src/lib.rs ===\n346 wasm/src/lib.rs\n//! SmolLM2 WASM inference library for browser-based LLM.\n//!\n//! This module provides WebAssembly bindings for running SmolLM2-135M\n//! language model inference directly in the browser without server processing.\n\nuse candle_core::{Device, Tensor};\nuse candle_nn::VarBuilder;\nuse candle_transformers::generation::LogitsProcessor;\nuse candle_transformers::models::llama::{Cache, Config, Llama, LlamaConfig};\nuse serde::{Deserialize, Serialize};\nuse std::sync::Mutex;\nuse tokenizers::Tokenizer;\nuse wasm_bindgen::prelude::*;\n\n// Console logging for debugging\n#[wasm_bindgen]\nextern \"C\" {\n    #[wasm_bindgen(js_namespace = console)]\n    fn log(s: &str);\n    #[wasm_bindgen(js_namespace = console)]\n    fn error(s: &str);\n}\n\n/// Macro for console logging in WASM context.\nmacro_rules! console_log {\n    ($($t:tt)*) => Models.dev is created by the maintainers of SST.\n\n## Key Features\n\nThe homepage starts with provider-agnostic model metadata, with model pages listing the providers serving that model, provider pages listing every model available from that provider, and lab pages grouping canonical models by author.\n\nYou can access provider data, provider-agnostic model metadata, or the combined catalog through JSON endpoints.\n\n## Data Structure\n\nThe data is stored in the repo as TOML files; organized by provider and model. The system serves model metadata through a public JSON API and an interactive web interface, with data stored in TOML files validated by Zod schemas and deployed to Cloudflare's edge network.\n\n## Community Contribution\n\nModels.dev was started as a community-contributed project to address the lack of a centralized database for AI model information. The project welcomes contributions from the community to keep the model data up to date.\n\n\nREMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks."

[2026-06-09T22:49:45.213Z] [INFO]         "content": "# Models.dev Repository Overview\n\n## Maintainer & License\nCreated by the maintainers of SST, this project is licensed under MIT. The repository has 4.9k stars and 1.1k forks on GitHub.\n\n## Purpose\nModels.dev is \"a comprehensive open-source database of AI model specifications, pricing, and capabilities.\" It was launched to address the lack of a unified resource documenting available AI models and serves as both a community project and internal tool for opencode.\n\n## Data Structure\n\n**File Format & Organization:**\n- Data uses TOML files organized hierarchically by provider and model\n- Model-only metadata lives in `models/` directory\n- Provider-specific details live in `providers/{provider}/models/` directories\n- If a model ID contains `/`, subfolders are used (e.g., `providers/openai/models/gpt-5.toml`)\n\n**Model Entry Fields:**\nEssential fields include name, release_date, last_updated, and capability flags (attachment, reasoning, tool_call, structured_output, temperature). Nested tables cover cost (input/output pricing), limits (context/input/output tokens), and modalities (input/output types). Optional fields include knowledge cutoff date, status, and benchmarks.\n\n## Contributing New Models\n\nContributors should:\n1. Create a provider folder if needed with `provider.toml`\n2. Optionally add an SVG `logo.svg`\n3. Create model TOML files with required specifications\n4. Submit a pull request; automated validation checks TOML syntax and schema compliance\n\n## API Access\nThree endpoints are available: `/api.json` (provider-specific data), `/models.json` (provider-agnostic metadata), and `/catalog.json` (combined response). Provider logos are served as SVGs at `/logos/{provider}.svg`."

Transformers.js is a JavaScript library from Hugging Face that allows you to run pre-trained AI models directly in the browser or in a JS runtime (Node.js, bun, Deno, etc.).\n\n## Key Organizations\n\nKey organizations include onnx-community (optimized ONNX exports, recommended for v4) and Xenova (legacy ONNX models, still compatible).\n\n## Model Formats\n\nCurrently, transformers.js works with ONNX models. There has been a feature request for GGUF support, as GGUF files offer wider support and ONNX doesn't quantize below 8bit, but GGUF does.\n\n## Finding Compatible Models\n\nTo find compatible models on the Hub, select the \"transformers.js\" library tag in the filter menu (or visit this link).\n\n## Recent Developments\n\nThe library has evolved significantly, with the biggest change being the adoption of a new WebGPU Runtime, completely rewritten in C++. Additionally, Transformers.js will now be published under the official Hugging Face organization on NPM as @huggingface/transformers (instead of @xenova/transformers, which was used for v1 and v2).\n\n\nREMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks."

Here are the key findings:\n\n## Resolve URL Pattern and Downloads\n\nURLs containing a `/resolve/` segment in their path serve user-generated content from the Hub, and are the URLs constructed by open source libraries (transformers, datasets, vLLM, llama.cpp, …) or AI applications to download model/dataset files from HF.\n\n## Rate Limits\n\nHugging Face enforces rate limits on all requests made to the Hub, with different rate limits defined for distinct classes of requests. Resolver rate limits are much higher and much more optimized compared to standard Hub API calls.\n\nThe huggingface_hub library (version 1.2.0+) includes smart retry handling for rate limit errors - when a 429 error occurs, the SDK automatically parses the RateLimit header to extract the exact number of seconds until the rate limit resets, then waits precisely that duration before retrying.\n\n## CORS and Browser Fetch Issues\n\nThere are documented CORS challenges with browser-based access. Downloads from the Hub redirect to cas-bridge.xethub.hf.co (Xet bridge), but the redirected host fails CORS preflight for Range/HEAD workflows, blocking partial reads. Additionally, simple GET requests work fine through the CDN redirect but Range requests fail with CORS preflight errors when redirected to cas-bridge.xethub.hf.co.\n\n\nREMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks."

Support tables for HTML5, CSS3, etc\",\"url\":\"https://caniuse.com/mdn-api_navigator_devicememory\"}]\n\nBased on the search results, here's comprehensive information about `navigator.deviceMemory`:\n\n## Units and Possible Values\n\nThe property returns a floating point number representing one of: 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, or 8 GiB.\n\n## How Values Are Calculated\n\nThe value is approximated by rounding the actual memory to the nearest power of 2, then dividing that number by 1024, and is then clamped within lower and upper bounds to protect the privacy of owners of very low-memory or high-memory devices.\n\n## Privacy Considerations\n\nThe reported value is imprecise to curtail fingerprinting. To reduce fingerprinting risk, the reported value is rounded to single most significant bit, and an upper and lower bound is placed on the reported values.\n\n## Browser Support\n\nnavigator.deviceMemory is Chromium-only and works in Chrome, Edge, Opera, and Brave, while Firefox and Safari don't implement it. Chromium browsers also send a Device-Memory HTTP header with requests if the server opts in via the Accept-CH response header.\n\n\nREMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks."

[2026-06-09T22:49:55.057Z] [INFO]         "tool_use_id": "toolu_012qKuciWvrrugiCKriNcEkM",
[2026-06-09T22:49:55.057Z] [INFO]         "type": "tool_result",
[2026-06-09T22:49:55.057Z] [INFO]         "content": "# Hub Rate limits\n\nTo protect our platform's integrity and ensure availability to as many AI community members as possible, we enforce rate limits on all requests made to the Hugging Face Hub.\n\nWe define different rate limits for distinct classes of requests. We distinguish three main buckets:\n\n- **Hub APIs**\n  - e.g. model or dataset search, repo creation, user management, etc. All endpoints that belong to this bucket are documented in [Hub API Endpoints](./api).\n- **Resolvers**\n  - They're all the URLs that contain a `/resolve/` segment in their path, which serve user-generated content from the Hub. Concretely, those are the URLs that are constructed by open source libraries (transformers, datasets, vLLM, llama.cpp, …) or AI applications (LM Studio, Jan, ollama, …) to download model/dataset files from HF.\n  - Specifically, this is the [\"Resolve a file\" endpoint](https://huggingface-openapi.hf.space/#tag/models/get/apiresolve-cachemodelsnamespacereporevpath) documented in our OpenAPI spec.\n  - Resolve requests are heavily used by the community, and since we optimize our infrastructure to serve them with maximum efficiency, the rate limits for Resolvers are the highest.\n- **Pages**\n  - All the Web pages we host on huggingface.co.\n  - Usually Web browsing requests are made by humans, hence rate limits don't need to be as high as the above mentioned programmatic endpoints.\n\n> [!TIP]\n> All values are defined over 5-minute windows, which allows for some level of \"burstiness\" from an application or developer's point of view.\n\nIf you, your organization, or your application need higher rate limits, we encourage you to upgrade your account to PRO, Team, or Enterprise. We prioritize support requests from PRO, Team, and Enterprise customers – see built-in limits in [Rate limit Tiers](#rate-limit-tiers).\n\n## Billing dashboard\n\nAt any point, you can check your rate limit status on your (or your org’s) Billing page: https://huggingface.co/settings/billing\n\n![dashboard for rate limits](https://cdn-uploads.huggingface.co/production/uploads/5dd96eb166059660ed1ee413/0pzQQyuVG3c9tWjCqrX9Y.png)\n\nOn the right side, you will see three gauges, one for each bucket of Requests.\n\nEach bucket presents the number of current (last 5 minutes) requests, and the number of allowed requests based on your user account or organization plan.\n\nWhenever you exceed the limit in the past 5 minutes (the view is updated in real-time), the bar will turn red.\n\nNote: You can use the context switcher to easily switch between your user account and your orgs.\n\n## HTTP Headers\n\nWhenever you or your organization hits a rate limit, you will receive a **429** `Too Many Requests` HTTP error.\n\nWe implement the mechanism described in the [IETF draft (Version 9)](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-ietf-httpapi-ratelimit-headers/) titled “RateLimit HTTP header fields for HTTP” (also known as `draft-ietf-httpapi-ratelimit-headers`).\n\nThe goal is to define standardized HTTP headers that servers can use to advertise quota / rate-limit policies and communicate current usage / limits to clients so that they can avoid being throttled.\n\nPrecisely, we implement the following headers:\n\n| Header                 | Purpose / Meaning                                                                                                                       |\n| ---------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |\n| **`RateLimit`**        | The total allowed rate limit for the current window. “How many requests (of this type) you’re allowed to perform.”                      |\n| **`RateLimit-Policy`** | Carries the rate limit policy itself (e.g. “100 requests per 5 minutes”). It’s informative; shows what policy the client is subject to. |\n\nA set of examples is as follows:\n\n| Header                 | Example                                                                                               |\n| ---------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |\n| **`RateLimit`**        | `\"api\\|pages\\|resolvers\";r=[remaining];t=[seconds remaining until reset]`                             |\n| **`RateLimit-Policy`** | `\"fixed window\";\"api\\|\\pages\\|resolvers\";q=[total allowed for window];w=[window duration in seconds]` |\n\n## Rate limit Tiers\n\nHere are the current rate limits (in September '25) based on your plan:\n\n| Plan                                                                      | API      | Resolvers | Pages  |\n| ------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------- | --------- | ------ |\n| Anonymous user (per IP address)                                           | 500 \\*   | 3,000 \\*  | 100 \\* |\n| Free user                                                                 | 1,000 \\* | 5,000 \\*  | 200 \\* |\n| PRO user                                                                  | 2,500    | 12,000    | 400    |\n| Team organization                                                         | 3,000    | 20,000    | 400    |\n| Enterprise organization                                                   | 6,000    | 50,000    | 600    |\n| Enterprise Plus organization                                              | 10,000   | 100,000   | 1,000  |\n| Enterprise Plus organization  When Organization IP Ranges are defined | 100,000  | 500,000   | 10,000 |\n| Academia Hub organization                                                 | 3,000    | 20,000    | 400    |\n\n\\* Anonymous and Free users are subject to change over time depending on platform health 🤞\n\n> [!NOTE]\n> All quotas are calculated over 5-minute fixed windows.\n\nNote: For organizations, rate limits are applied individually to each member, not shared among members.\n\n## What if I get rate-limited\n\nFirst, make sure you always pass a `HF_TOKEN`, and it is passed downstream to all libraries or applications that download _stuff_ from the Hub.\n\nThis is the number one reason users get rate limited and is a very easy fix.\n\nDespite passing `HF_TOKEN` if you are still rate limited, you can:\n\n- spread out your requests over longer periods of time\n- replace Hub API calls with Resolver calls, whenever possible (Resolver rate limits are much higher and much more optimized).\n- upgrade to PRO, Team, or Enterprise.\n\n## Smart rate limit handling with `huggingface_hub`\n\nThe Hub Python Library [`huggingface_hub`](https://huggingface.co/docs/huggingface_hub/index) (version **1.2.0+**) includes smart retry handling for rate limit errors.\n\nWhen a 429 error occurs, the SDK automatically parses the `RateLimit` header to extract the exact number of seconds until the rate limit resets, then waits precisely that duration before retrying. This applies to file downloads (i.e. Resolvers) and paginated Hub API calls (list models, datasets, spaces, etc.).\n\n**We strongly recommend using `huggingface_hub` for all programmatic access to the Hub** to benefit from this optimized retry behavior and avoid implementing custom rate limit handling.\n\n## Granular user action Rate limits\n\nIn addition to those main classes of rate limits, we enforce limits on certain specific kinds of user actions, like:\n\n- repo creation\n- repo commits\n- discussions and comments\n- moderation actions\n- etc.\n\nWe don't currently document the rate limits for those specific actions, given they tend to change over time more often. If you get quota errors, we encourage you to upgrade your account to PRO, Team, or Enterprise.\nFeel free to get in touch with us via the support team.\n\n"

[2026-06-09T22:49:56.695Z] [INFO]         "content": "# Search the Hub\n\nIn this tutorial, you will learn how to search models, datasets and spaces on the Hub using `huggingface_hub`.\n\n## How to list repositories ?\n\n`huggingface_hub` library includes an HTTP client [HfApi](/docs/huggingface_hub/v1.16.2/en/package_reference/hf_api#huggingface_hub.HfApi) to interact with the Hub.\nAmong other things, it can list models, datasets and spaces stored on the Hub:\n\n```py\n>>> from huggingface_hub import HfApi\n>>> api = HfApi()\n>>> models = api.list_models()\n```\n\nThe output of [list_models()](/docs/huggingface_hub/v1.16.2/en/package_reference/hf_api#huggingface_hub.HfApi.list_models) is an iterator over the models stored on the Hub.\n\nSimilarly, you can use [list_datasets()](/docs/huggingface_hub/v1.16.2/en/package_reference/hf_api#huggingface_hub.HfApi.list_datasets) to list datasets and [list_spaces()](/docs/huggingface_hub/v1.16.2/en/package_reference/hf_api#huggingface_hub.HfApi.list_spaces) to list Spaces.\n\n## How to filter repositories ?\n\nListing repositories is great but now you might want to filter your search.\nThe list helpers have several attributes like:\n- `filter`\n- `author`\n- `search`\n- `num_parameters`\n- ...\n\nLet's see an example to get all models on the Hub that does image classification, have been trained on the imagenet dataset and that runs with PyTorch.\n\n```py\nmodels = hf_api.list_models(filter=[\"image-classification\", \"pytorch\", \"imagenet\"])\n```\n\nYou can also filter models by parameter count using the same range syntax as the Hub UI:\n\n```py\nmodels = hf_api.list_models(num_parameters=\"min:6B,max:128B\")\n```\n\nWhile filtering, you can also sort the models and take only the top results. For example,\nthe following example fetches the top 5 most downloaded datasets on the Hub:\n\n```py\n>>> list(list_datasets(sort=\"downloads\", limit=5))\n[DatasetInfo(\n\tid='argilla/databricks-dolly-15k-curated-en',\n\tauthor='argilla',\n\tsha='4dcd1dedbe148307a833c931b21ca456a1fc4281',\n\tlast_modified=datetime.datetime(2023, 10, 2, 12, 32, 53, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc),\n\tprivate=False,\n\tdownloads=8889377,\n\t(...)\n```\n\nTo explore available filters on the Hub, visit [models](https://huggingface.co/models) and [datasets](https://huggingface.co/datasets) pages\nin your browser, search for some parameters and look at the values in the URL.\n\n## Using the CLI\n\nYou can also list and search for models, datasets, and Spaces using the `hf` command-line interface:\n\n```bash\n# List models\n>>> hf models ls --search \"llama\" --sort downloads --limit 5\n\n# List datasets\n>>> hf datasets ls --author Qwen\n\n# List Spaces\n>>> hf spaces ls --search \"3d\"\n\n# Get info about a specific model\n>>> hf models info Lightricks/LTX-2\n```\n\nFor more details, see the [CLI guide](./cli#hf-models).\n\n"

The API is available only for cross-origin isolated web pages that opt in using the COOP+COEP headers preventing cross-origin information leaks.\n\n## performance.memory (Legacy API)\n\nThe search results address the relationship between the new `measureUserAgentSpecificMemory()` and the older `performance.memory` API. If you are familiar with the existing non-standard performance.memory API, you might be wondering how the new API differs from it. The main difference is that the old API returns the size of the JavaScript heap whereas the new API estimates the memory used by the web page.\n\nAdditionally, performance.memory (legacy API) cannot be used for this purpose as it was never enabled on workers.\n\n## Current Browser Support\n\nCurrently the API is supported only in Chromium-based browsers, starting in Chrome 89.\n\nThe search results don't explicitly mention `performance.memory` being deprecated, but they indicate that `measureUserAgentSpecificMemory()` is the modern replacement for memory measurement in web applications.\n\n\nREMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks."

In WGSL, the f16 type is the set of 16-bit floating-point values of the IEEE-754 binary16 format, using 16 bits to represent a floating-point number as opposed to 32 bits for conventional single-precision floating-point (f32).\n\n**Requesting Features and Limits:**\nYou can request features and limits when you call requestDevice by passing your desired limits as requiredLimits and your desired features as requiredFeatures.\n\nThe search results don't provide specific distinctions between mobile and desktop limits, though they mention that limit values vary by hardware and browser implementation.\n\n\nREMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks."

Here's a summary of the key findings:\n\n## Storage Quota Management with navigator.storage.estimate()\n\nWeb developers can check how much space is available for their origin and how much is being used by the origin with the navigator.storage.estimate() method of the Storage API. The promise that estimate() returns resolves with an object containing two properties: usage, representing the number of bytes currently used, and quota, representing the maximum bytes that can be stored by the current origin.\n\n## Supported Storage APIs\n\nAttempting to store more than an origin's quota using IndexedDB, Cache, or OPFS, for example, fails with a QuotaExceededError exception. The data stored for a website which is managed by the Storage Standard usually includes IndexedDB databases and Cache API data, but may include other kind of site-accessible data such as Web Storage API data.\n\n## Persistence and Eviction\n\nIf, for any reason, developers need persistent storage (e.g., when building a web app that relies on critical data that isn't persisted anywhere else), they can do so by using the navigator.storage.persist() method of the Storage API. By default, all origins use a best-effort mode, which means their persistence is not guaranteed and their data can be evicted.\n\n## Typical Quota\n\nQuota varies by browser: Chrome (and Chromium-based browsers) typically allow you to use a percentage of the user's free disk space, whereas Firefox historically prompts users to allow more than 5 MB in mobile or 50 MB in desktop. In Microsoft Edge, local and session storage are limited to about 5MB each. Other types of data storage, such as IndexedDB, Cache API, or Origin Private File System Access API, can use up to 60% of the total disk space on the device.\n\n\nREMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks."

Low-end devices with 4GB total RAM hit practical limits quickly with WebGPU and larger models.\n\n## Model Sizing Guidance\n\nModels ≤2B parameters are limited to interactive speeds on mainstream hardware, and quantized models under 2GB run at interactive speeds on consumer hardware. For reference, Phi-3-mini at ~3.8B parameters requires substantially more VRAM; budget 4 to 8GB of GPU memory for FP16.\n\n## Memory Optimization through Quantization\n\nA model stored in FP32 takes four bytes per parameter. Move to INT8 (q8), and it's one byte – 4x smaller with minimal quality loss. Move to INT4 (q4), and it's half a byte – 8x smaller.\n\n## WASM vs WebGPU\n\nTransformers.js converts Hugging Face models to ONNX format and executes them through ONNX Runtime Web. When using the WASM backend, inference runs entirely on the CPU. Choosing incorrectly between WebGPU and WebAssembly can mean a 10 to 15x performance difference depending on model size, task shape, and hardware.\n\nThe key takeaway is that the constraint that isn't going away is memory.\n\n\nREMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks."

[2026-06-09T22:50:02.765Z] [INFO]         "content": "=== README (head) ===\n<p align=\"center\">\n  <a href=\"https://models.dev\">\n    <picture>\n      <source srcset=\"./logo-dark.svg\" media=\"(prefers-color-scheme: dark)\">\n      <source srcset=\"./logo-light.svg\" media=\"(prefers-color-scheme: light)\">\n      <img src=\"./logo-light.svg\" alt=\"Models.dev logo\">\n    </picture>\n  </a>\n</p>\n\n---\n\n[Models.dev](https://models.dev) is a comprehensive open-source database of AI model specifications, pricing, and capabilities.\n\nThere's no single database with information about all the available AI models. We started Models.dev as a community-contributed project to address this. We also use it internally in [opencode](https://opencode.ai).\n\n## API\n\nYou can access this data through an API.\n\n```bash\ncurl https://models.dev/api.json\n```\n\nUse the **Model ID** field to do a lookup on any model; it's the identifier used by [AI SDK](https://ai-sdk.dev/).\n\nProvider-agnostic model metadata is available separately:\n\n```bash\ncurl https://models.dev/models.json\n```\n\nUse this for facts about the model itself, independent of where it is served. If you need both provider endpoints and model-only metadata in one response:\n\n```bash\ncurl https://models.dev/catalog.json\n```\n\n### Logos\n\nProvider logos are available as SVG files:\n\n```bash\ncurl https://models.dev/logos/{provider}.svg\n```\n\nReplace `{provider}` with the **Provider ID** (e.g., `anthropic`, `openai`, `google`). If we don't have a provider's logo, a default logo is served instead.\n\n## Contributing\n\nThe data is stored in the repo as TOML files; organized by provider and model. The logo is stored as an SVG. This is used to generate this page and power the API.\n\nWe need your help keeping the data up to date.\n\n### Adding Model Metadata\n\nModel-only facts live in `models/`, using the same path-style IDs as provider models. For example, `models/openai/gpt-5.toml` defines metadata for the underlying GPT-5 model, while `providers/openai/models/gpt-5.toml` defines OpenAI-specific serving details such as pricing.\n\nUse model metadata for provider-agnostic facts:\n\n- `name`, `family`, `release_date`, `last_updated`, `knowledge`\n- `attachment`, `reasoning`, `tool_call`, `structured_output`, `temperature`\n- `[limit]` defaults like context, input, and output token limits\n- `[modalities]` defaults\n- `open_weights`, `license`, `links`, `weights`, and `benchmarks`\n\nExample:\n\n```toml\nname = \"GPT-5\"\nfamily = \"gpt\"\nrelease_date = \"2025-08-07\"\nlast_updated = \"2025-08-07\"\nattachment = true\nreasoning = true\ntemperature = false\ntool_call = true\nstructured_output = true\nopen_weights = false\n\n[limit]\ncontext = 400_000\ninput = 272_000\noutput = 128_000\n\n[modalities]\ninput = [\"text\", \"image\"]\noutput = [\"text\"]\n\n[[benchmarks]]\nname = \"Benchmark Name\"\nscore = 72.5\nmetric = \"accuracy\"\nsource = \"https://example.com/results\"\n\n[[weights]]\nlabel = \"Model weights\"\nurl = \"https://huggingface.co/example/model\"\nformat = \"safetensors\"\n```\n\nProvider TOMLs can inherit these facts with `base_model` and then keep only provider-specific fields or overrides:\n\n```toml\nbase_model = \"openai/gpt-5\"\n\n[cost]\ninput = 1.25\noutput = 10.00\ncache_read = 0.125\n\n[limit]\ncontext = 200_000 # optional provider override\noutput = 32_000\n```\n\nProvider fields win over model metadata during generation. Use this when the underlying model is the same but a provider serves it with different context limits, modalities, features, or pricing.\n\n### Adding a New Provider Model",

[2026-06-09T22:50:05.471Z] [INFO]         "content": "To add a new model, start by checking if the provider already exists in the `providers/` directory. If not, then:\n\n#### 1. Create a Provider\n\nIf the provider isn't already in `providers/`:\n\n1. Create a new folder in `providers/` with the provider's ID. For example, `providers/newprovider/`.\n2. Add a `provider.toml` with the provider details:\n\n   ```toml\n   name = \"Provider Name\"\n   npm = \"@ai-sdk/provider\" # AI SDK Package name\n   env = [\"PROVIDER_API_KEY\"] # Environment Variable keys used for auth\n   doc = \"https://example.com/docs/models\" # Link to provider's documentation\n   ```\n\n   If the provider doesn’t publish an npm package but exposes an OpenAI-compatible endpoint, set the npm field accordingly and include the base URL:\n\n   ```toml\n   npm = \"@ai-sdk/openai-compatible\" # Use OpenAI-compatible SDK\n   api = \"https://api.example.com/v1\" # Required with openai-compatible\n   ```\n\n#### 2. Add a Logo (optional)\n\nTo add a logo for the provider:\n\n1. Add a `logo.svg` file to the provider's directory (e.g., `providers/newprovider/logo.svg`)\n2. Use SVG format with no fixed size or colors - use `currentColor` for fills/strokes\n\nExample SVG structure:\n\n```svg\n<svg xmlns=\"http://www.w3.org/2000/svg\" viewBox=\"0 0 24 24\" fill=\"currentColor\">\n  <!-- Logo paths here -->\n</svg>\n```\n\n#### 3. Add a Model Definition\n\nCreate a new TOML file in the provider's `models/` directory where the filename is the model ID.\n\nIf the model ID contains `/`, use subfolders. For example, for the model ID `openai/gpt-5`, create a folder `openai/` and place a file named `gpt-5.toml` inside it.\n\n```toml\nname = \"Model Display Name\"\nattachment = true           # or false - supports file attachments\nreasoning = false           # or true - supports reasoning / chain-of-thought\ntool_call = true            # or false - supports tool calling\nstructured_output = true    # or false - supports a dedicated structured output feature\ntemperature = true          # or false - supports temperature control\nknowledge = \"2024-04\"       # Knowledge-cutoff date\nrelease_date = \"2025-02-19\" # First public release date\nlast_updated = \"2025-02-19\" # Most recent update date\nopen_weights = true         # or false  - model’s trained weights are publicly available\n\n[cost]\ninput = 3.00                # Cost per million input tokens (USD)\noutput = 15.00              # Cost per million output tokens (USD)\nreasoning = 15.00           # Cost per million reasoning tokens (USD)\ncache_read = 0.30           # Cost per million cached read tokens (USD)\ncache_write = 3.75          # Cost per million cached write tokens (USD)\ninput_audio = 1.00          # Cost per million audio input tokens (USD)\noutput_audio = 10.00        # Cost per million audio output tokens (USD)\n\n[limit]\ncontext = 400_000           # Maximum context window (tokens)\ninput = 272_000             # Maximum input tokens\noutput = 8_192              # Maximum output tokens\n\n[modalities]\ninput = [\"text\", \"image\"]   # Supported input modalities\noutput = [\"text\"]           # Supported output modalities\n\n[interleaved]\nfield = \"reasoning_content\" # Name of the interleaved field \"reasoning_content\" or \"reasoning_details\"\n```\n\n#### 3a. Reuse Model Metadata with `base_model`\n\nFor wrapper providers that mirror an existing model, prefer referencing the model-only metadata instead of duplicating provider-agnostic fields.\n\nUse `base_model` when the provider serves the same underlying model and only provider-specific fields differ.\n\n```toml\nbase_model = \"anthropic/claude-opus-4-6\"\n\n[cost]\ninput = 5.00\noutput = 25.00\n```\n\nRules:\n\n- `base_model` must point to a TOML file in `models/` using `<provider>/<model-id>`.\n- You can override any top-level model field locally.\n- If you override a nested table like `[cost]`, `[limit]`, or `[modalities]`, include the full values needed for that table.\n- `base_model_omit` is optional and removes inherited model metadata fields after local overrides are merged. Use dot-path strings, for example `base_model_omit = [\"limit.input\"]`.\n- `id` still comes from the filename; do not add it to the TOML.\n\nUse `base_model` when the wrapper model is materially the same as the source model and only differs by provider-specific pricing, limits, modalities, provider request shape, or lifecycle flags.\n\nSync and generator scripts should preserve existing `base_model` / `base_model_omit` fields when updating provider TOMLs. Do not use legacy `[extends]` tables.\n\n#### 4. Submit a Pull Request\n\n1. Fork this repo\n2. Create a new branch with your changes\n3. Add your provider and/or model files\n4. Open a PR with a clear description\n\n### Validation\n\nThere's a GitHub Action that will automatically validate your submission against our schema to ensure:\n\n- All required fields are present\n- Data types are correct\n- Values are within acceptable ranges\n- TOML syntax is valid\n\nWhen moving existing provider fields into model metadata, compare generated output before and after the change:\n\n```bash\nbun run compare:migrations\n```\n\nThis prints a diff for each changed model TOML so you can confirm the generated JSON only changed where you intended.\n\n### Schema Reference\n\nModels must conform to the following schema, as defined in `packages/core/src/schema.ts`.\n\n**Provider Schema:**\n\n- `name`: String - Display name of the provider\n- `npm`: String - AI SDK Package name\n- `env`: String[] - Environment variable keys used for auth\n- `doc`: String - Link to the provider's documentation\n- `api` _(optional)_: String - OpenAI-compatible API endpoint. Required only when using `@ai-sdk/openai-compatible` as the npm package",

Here's a summary of the results:\n\n## Overview\n\nWllama is a WebAssembly binding for llama.cpp that can run completely on browser. It's a project by ngxson that enables browser-based LLM inference.\n\n## Key Features\n\n- Wllama is a WebAssembly library for llama.cpp, supporting SIMD and multi-threaded inference of GGUF models in browsers, featuring a JavaScript API for loading models from Hugging Face or URLs, with tools to split large models for efficient memory use\n- Runs inference in a Web Worker for a smooth UI\n\n## Model Splitting for GGUF Files\n\nIf your model is bigger than 2GB, you can split the model into small files, and even with a small model, splitting into chunks allows the browser to download multiple chunks in parallel, thus making the download process a bit faster.\n\nThe library uses llama-gguf-split to split a big gguf file into smaller files, outputting files ending with -00001-of-00003.gguf, -00002-of-00003.gguf, and so on.\n\n## Model Caching\n\nWllama includes a ModelManager that can list all models in cache, download new models, and validate whether a model is corrupted.\n\nThe project is available on GitHub at https://github.com/ngxson/wllama and on npm as `@wllama/wllama`.\n\n\nREMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks."

The library is maintained under the Hugging Face organization as @huggingface/transformers.\n\n## ONNX Runtime and Backend Options\n\nTransformers.js executes models using ONNX Runtime compiled for JavaScript environments. ONNX (Open Neural Network Exchange) acts as a universal format that lets models trained in PyTorch, TensorFlow, or JAX run in the browser.\n\n### WASM and WebGPU Support\n\nBy default, when running in the browser, the model will be run on your CPU (via WASM). If you would like to run the model on your GPU (via WebGPU), you can do this by setting device: 'webgpu'.\n\nThe biggest change is undoubtedly the adoption of a new WebGPU Runtime, completely rewritten in C++. We've worked closely with the ONNX Runtime team to thoroughly test this runtime across our ~200 supported model architectures, as well as many new v4-exclusive architectures.\n\n## Caching Pipeline\n\nenv.useWasmCache enables caching of WASM runtime files (when cache storage is available), allowing applications to work fully offline after the initial load. Model files are cached in the browser after the initial download. Once cached, inference can work without a network connection.\n\n## Pipeline API\n\nJust like the python library, we support the pipeline API. Pipelines group together a pretrained model with preprocessing of inputs and postprocessing of outputs, making it the easiest way to run models with the library.\n\n\nREMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks."

Ratchet is lazy, no computation is done until the entire computation graph is built and executed, which aligns closely with CUDAGraphs & Command buffers.\n\n**picoLLM (Picovoice)**\npicoLLM Compression is a novel large language model quantization algorithm developed within Picovoice that automatically learns the optimal bit allocation strategy across and within LLM's weights. Picovoice developed picoLLM Compression and the picoLLM Inference Engine to create the first-ever cross-browser local LLM inference engine. WebGPU doesn't work across all major browsers, but picoLLM Inference Engine is not only cross-browser compatible but also speedy.\n\n## WebGPU Technology\n\nWebGPU is a new web standard for accelerated graphics and compute that enables web developers to use the underlying system's GPU to carry out high-performance computations directly in the browser. As of October 2024, global WebGPU support is around 70%.\n\n## Browser Inference Benefits\n\nWith WebGPU, you can ship a local LLM that runs inside a tab — no servers, no API keys, and no data leaving the device.\n\n\nREMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks."

Everything runs inside the browser with no server support and is accelerated with WebGPU.\n\n## prebuiltAppConfig and model_id\n\nEach of the model in WebLLM Chat is registered as an instance of ModelRecord and can be accessed at webllm.prebuiltAppConfig.model_list. WebLLM supports a subset of these available models and the list can be accessed at prebuiltAppConfig.model_list.\n\nTo use a model with WebLLM, you need to configure an AppConfig with model_id (e.g., \"Llama-3-8B-Instruct-q4f32_1-MLC\"), the model URL, and the model_lib which is the WebGPU WASM library.\n\n## VRAM Requirements\n\nThe larger model might take more VRAM, try start with smaller models first. For practical guidance, if VRAM is greater than 8GB, load Llama-3-8B; if it's only 4GB, downgrade to load Phi-3-Mini-4K-Instruct.\n\nIf the model fails to load with an Out of Memory (OOM) prompt, this is usually because the user's VRAM is insufficient to hold the selected model. You should choose a smaller level of quantization model (such as q3f16 or q4f16) when initializing CreateMLCEngine.\n\n## Quantization and Model Catalog\n\nq4f16_1 is recommended for most applications. The supported models include various families like Llama, Phi, Gemma, Mistral, and others available through the MLC Models catalog.\n\n\nREMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks."

Modern CPUs can run multiple threads per physical core through advanced scheduling.\n\n### Example Return Value\nA four-core CPU typically reports **8 logical processors** (2 logical cores per physical core).\n\n### Important Caveat\n> \"The browser may, however, choose to report a lower number of logical cores in order to represent more accurately the number of [`Worker`](/en-US/docs/Web/API/Worker)s that can run at once, so don't treat this as an absolute measurement of the number of cores in the user's system.\"\n\n## Code Example\n```javascript\nlet workerList = [];\n\nfor (let i = 0; i < window.navigator.hardwareConcurrency; i++) {\n  let newWorker = {\n    worker: new Worker(\"cpu-worker.js\"),\n    inUse: false,\n  };\n  workerList.push(newWorker);\n}\n```\n\n## Browser Support\n- **Baseline:** Widely available\n- **Available since:** March 2022 across major browsers\n- Status: Well established and works across many devices and browser versions\n\n## Additional Notes\n- No secure context requirement mentioned in documentation\n- No explicit privacy considerations discussed in the provided content\n- Specified in the [HTML Standard](https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/workers.html#dom-navigator-hardwareconcurrency-dev)"

[2026-06-09T22:50:21.446Z] [INFO]         "content": "# Storage Quotas and Eviction Criteria - Key Facts\n\n## Storage Types & Persistence Modes\n\n**Two storage modes exist:**\n- **Best-effort** (default): Data persists while origin stays below quota and device has space; can be evicted without user interruption\n- **Persistent**: Only evicted if user manually deletes via browser settings; requires `navigator.storage.persist()` call\n\n## Storage Technologies Covered\n\n- IndexedDB\n- Cache API\n- Origin Private File System (OPFS)\n- WebAssembly code caching\n\n*(Note: Web Storage/localStorage and sessionStorage have separate limits)*\n\n## Browser-Specific Quota Limits\n\n### Firefox\n**Best-effort mode:**\n- Whichever is smaller: **10% of total disk** OR **10 GiB** (group limit per site)\n\n**Persistent mode:**\n- Up to **50% of total disk**, capped at 8 TiB; exempt from group limit\n\nExample: On 500 GiB drive → 10 GiB best-effort OR 250 GiB persistent\n\n### Chrome & Chromium-based (Chrome, Edge)\n- **60% of total disk** (both persistent and best-effort modes)\n\nExample: On 1 TiB drive → 600 GiB quota\n\n### Safari/WebKit\n**Browser apps (Safari and WebKit-based browsers):**\n- Up to **~60% of total disk**\n\n**Other apps with embedded web content:**\n- Up to **~15% of total disk**\n- Web app shortcuts: same as browser app quota (~60%)\n\n**Overall limit:** \n- Browser apps: **80% of disk** across all origins\n- Non-browser apps: **20% of disk** across all origins\n\n**Cross-origin frames:** ~1/10 of parent quota\n\n*Earlier Safari versions: 1 GiB initial quota with user permission prompts*\n\n## Checking Available Space\n\n```javascript\nconst estimate = await navigator.storage.estimate();\n// estimate.usage = data currently used\n// estimate.quota = maximum allowed\n```\n\n## Data Eviction Criteria\n\n### Storage Pressure Eviction\nWhen device runs low on space: **Least Recently Used (LRU) policy** deletes oldest unused origins first. Skips persistent origins.\n\n### Browser Maximum Exceeded\nChrome uses max **80% of total disk**. When all origins exceed this collective limit, best-effort origins evicted via LRU.\n\n### Proactive Eviction (Safari only)\nData from origins with **no user interaction for 7 days** is deleted (when cross-site tracking prevention enabled). Server-set cookies exempt.\n\n## Critical Behaviors\n\n**Complete deletion:** When eviction occurs, **all** origin data is deleted at once (IndexedDB AND Cache API AND OPFS together) to prevent inconsistency.\n\n**Quota calculation:** Based on hard drive **total size**, not available space, to prevent fingerprinting.\n\n**Web Storage separate:** localStorage/sessionStorage limited to **10 MiB maximum** (5 MiB each), throws `QuotaExceededError` when exceeded."

[2026-06-09T22:50:34.891Z] [INFO]         "content": "# measureUserAgentSpecificMemory() - Key Facts\n\n## Return Value Structure\n\nThe method returns a **Promise that resolves to an object** with this structure:\n\n```javascript\n{\n  \"bytes\": 1500000,\n  \"breakdown\": [\n    {\n      \"bytes\": 1000000,\n      \"attribution\": [\n        {\n          \"url\": \"https://example.com\",\n          \"scope\": \"Window\"\n        }\n      ],\n      \"types\": [\"DOM\", \"JS\"]\n    }\n  ]\n}\n```\n\n**Return object properties:**\n- **`bytes`**: Total memory usage as a number\n- **`breakdown`**: Array of objects partitioning memory with:\n  - **`bytes`**: Size of memory for this entry\n  - **`attribution`**: Array containing `url`, `container` (with `id`, `src`), and `scope` (\"Window\", \"DedicatedWorkerGlobalScope\", \"SharedWorkerGlobalScope\", \"ServiceWorkerGlobalScope\", or \"cross-origin-aggregated\")\n  - **`types`**: Array of memory types (e.g., [\"DOM\", \"JS\"])\n\n## Security Requirements\n\n**Must satisfy both conditions:**\n\n1. **Secure context** (HTTPS only)\n2. **Cross-origin isolated** (COOP/COEP headers)\n\nVerification code:\n```javascript\nif (crossOriginIsolated) {\n  // Use measureUserAgentSpecificMemory\n}\n```\n\nThrows `SecurityError` DOMException if requirements aren't met.\n\n## Available Contexts\n\nPer documentation note: **\"This feature is available in Web Workers\"** plus the main window context.\n\n## Browser Support\n\nThe page indicates **\"Limited availability\"** and **\"Experimental\"** status, stating it \"does not work in some of the most widely-used browsers.\" The content does not specify Chrome 89+ explicitly, though this is the historical baseline.\n\n## Important Distinctions\n\nThe documentation **does NOT compare** `measureUserAgentSpecificMemory()` to the legacy `performance.memory` API. No direct comparison or differentiation is provided in this content.\n\n## Key Usage Pattern\n\n```javascript\nasync function measureMemory() {\n  const memorySample = await performance.measureUserAgentSpecificMemory();\n  console.log(memorySample);\n}\n```\n\n**Not meant for single calls** — the API is designed for **periodic measurement with randomized intervals** to detect memory regression and track changes over session duration."

[2026-06-09T22:50:36.994Z] [INFO]         "content": "# Window: crossOriginIsolated Property - Key Facts\n\n## What It Indicates\n\nThe `crossOriginIsolated` read-only property returns a **boolean** indicating whether a document is cross-origin isolated. A cross-origin isolated document:\n\n- Only shares its browsing context group with same-origin documents in popups and navigations\n- Can communicate via CORS (and COEP for `<iframe>`)\n- Severs relationships with cross-origin openers and popups\n- May be hosted in a separate OS process to mitigate side-channel and XS-Leaks attacks\n\n## Required HTTP Headers\n\nTo enable cross-origin isolation, a document must be served with **both**:\n\n### 1. Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy (COOP)\n```\nCross-Origin-Opener-Policy: same-origin\n```\n\n### 2. Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy (COEP)\n```\nCross-Origin-Embedder-Policy: require-corp\n```\nor\n```\nCross-Origin-Embedder-Policy: credentialless\n```\n\n## Gated APIs\n\nWhen `crossOriginIsolated` is `true`, these APIs operate with fewer restrictions:\n\n| API | Benefit |\n|-----|---------|\n| **`SharedArrayBuffer`** | Can be created and sent via `Window.postMessage()` or `MessagePort.postMessage()` |\n| **`Performance.now()`** | Offers better precision |\n| **`Performance.measureUserAgentSpecificMemory()`** | Can be called |\n\n## Code Example\n\n```javascript\nconst myWorker = new Worker(\"worker.js\");\n\nif (window.crossOriginIsolated) {\n  const buffer = new SharedArrayBuffer(16);\n  myWorker.postMessage(buffer);\n} else {\n  const buffer = new ArrayBuffer(16);\n  myWorker.postMessage(buffer);\n}\n```\n\n## Permissions-Policy\n\nThe `cross-origin-isolated` directive in the `Permissions-Policy` header must not block access (default allowlist is `self`, so permission is granted by default to cross-origin isolated documents).\n\n## Browser Support\n\nThe documentation references the [HTML specification](https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/webappapis.html#dom-crossoriginisolated-dev) but does not include explicit browser compatibility data in the provided content."

The implementation is straightforward: set `device: 'webgpu'` when loading models. According to the announcement, this delivers \"up to 100x faster than WASM\" performance.\n\nThe feature integrates with ONNX Runtime Web. As noted: *\"WebGPU is a new web standard for accelerated graphics and compute\"* that allows direct GPU access in browsers. However, browser support remains limited—approximately 70% globally as of October 2024.\n\n## Model Quantization (dtypes)\n\nThe release introduces flexible quantization options replacing the simple `quantized` boolean. Available formats include full-precision (`fp32`), half-precision (`fp16`), 8-bit variants, and 4-bit options like `q4`.\n\nFor sensitive models, developers can specify per-module quantization. The documentation demonstrates this with Florence-2: *\"some encoder-decoder models...are extremely sensitive to quantization settings\"*, allowing granular control over which modules use which precision levels.\n\n## Supported Architectures & Models\n\nThe update brings support to 120 architectures total, spanning models like Phi-3, Gemma 2, LLaVa, and MusicGen. Over 1,200 pre-converted models are available on the Hugging Face Hub, with a conversion script provided for custom models.\n\n## Runtime Compatibility\n\nTransformers.js v3 now works across Node.js (ESM and CommonJS), Deno, and Bun, expanding beyond browser-only usage to modern server-side JavaScript ecosystems."

Models registered via plugins become available through llm.get_model(model_id) and llm.get_async_model(model_id).\n\n## Local Model Catalog Plugins\n\nSeveral plugins are available for running LLMs directly on your own computer: llm-gguf uses llama.cpp to run models published in the GGUF format, llm-mlx (Mac only) uses Apple's MLX framework, llm-ollama adds support for local models run using Ollama, llm-llamafile adds support for local models using llamafile, llm-mlc can run local models released by the MLC project, and llm-gpt4all adds support for various models released by the GPT4All project.\n\n## Listing Available Models\n\nThe llm models -q openrouter command will display all available models. This pattern works for other plugins as well to display their available models.\n\n## Provider Plugins\n\nAdditional plugins support various API providers: llm-mistral adds support for Mistral AI's language and embedding models, llm-gemini adds support for Google's Gemini models, llm-anthropic supports Anthropic's Claude 3 family, llm-command-r supports Cohere's Command R and Command R Plus API models, and llm-reka supports the Reka family of models via their API.\n\n\nREMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks."

The documentation states: *\"WebGPU support is introduced via PR #215\"* and indicates it's automatically enabled in V3.1. Users can adjust GPU layer offloading via the `n_gpu_layers` parameter to manage VRAM constraints.\n\n## Caching Mechanism\nThe provided documentation doesn't mention ModelManager or the browser Cache API. There's no information about caching strategies in the content.\n\n## Streaming Token Output\nThe documentation doesn't explicitly address streaming token capabilities or real-time token generation features.\n\n## Key Limitations\n\n**Model Size:**\n- Maximum 2GB per file due to *\"size restriction of ArrayBuffer\"*\n- Larger models require splitting into chunks (recommended: max 512MB per file)\n\n**Threading Requirements:**\n- Multi-threading demands specific HTTP headers: *\"you must add `Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy` and `Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy` headers\"*\n\n**GPU Constraints:**\n- Models exceeding VRAM capacity require manual layer adjustment\n- Not all browsers support WebGPU (Firefox/Safari compatibility requires degraded \"compat mode\")\n\n**Operational Notes:**\n- While CPU/WASM inference works without backend infrastructure, it's inherently slower than GPU acceleration"

These aren't proposals anymore. They're production features.\n\n## Browser Support by Feature\n\n**Threads & SharedArrayBuffer:**\nThreading support via SharedArrayBuffer and atomics enables true parallel computation. Combined with SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data) operations, you can now run workloads that previously required native applications—video encoding, machine learning inference, and real-time audio processing.\n\n**Memory64:**\nBy the end of 2025, major browsers support all major features of WebAssembly 3.0, including Memory64 memory management, garbage collection, threads, SIMD, and exception handling. However, Memory64 is available in Chrome and Firefox but not yet in Safari.\n\n**SIMD:**\nThis proposal was standardized in 2024 but was still behind a flag in Firefox and Safari by the end of the year. In 2025, this feature came out from behind a flag in Firefox but is still behind a flag in Safari.\n\n## Key Requirements\n\nBrowser pthreads depend on shared memory, and shared memory depends on cross-origin isolation. In practice that means serving Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy: same-origin and Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy: require-corp or credentialless so SharedArrayBuffer is available.\n\n\nREMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks."

Let me provide you with the findings:\n\n## WebGPU maxBufferSize Limits and requestDevice\n\n### Default Limit Values\n\nThe default maxBufferSize device limit is 268435456 bytes (256 MB). This is the minimum guaranteed limit across all WebGPU-supporting devices.\n\n### Desktop Adapter Higher Limits\n\nDesktop adapters can support a higher maxBufferSize of 4294967296 bytes (4 GB), which can be specified in requiredLimits when calling requestDevice().\n\n### Mobile Device Limits\n\nFor mobile devices, the default fallback for maxBufferSize is 256MB, motivated by iOS Safari. Additionally, low-resource devices such as iOS and Android may not be able to support 1GB, with previous implementations falling back maxStorageBufferBindingSize to 128MB, the default values stated in WGSL doc, motivated by Android Chrome.\n\n### Using requestDevice with requiredLimits\n\nTo request higher limits, you need to explicitly specify them:\n\nError messages have been expanded with hints that tell you to explicitly request a higher limit when no limit was specified in requiredLimits when calling requestDevice().\n\n### Important Notes on Limit Reporting\n\nInstead of reporting the exact limits of each GPU, browsers typically report different tier values of different limits (to reduce the unique information available to drive-by fingerprinting). For example, the tiers of a certain limit might be 2048, 8192, and 32768. If your GPU's actual limit is 16384, the browser will still report 8192.\n\n\nREMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks."
