Synthesizing the final report.\n\n---\n\n# Browser Device-Capability Detection for Sizing In-Browser LLMs\n\nHow a web app can probe the host device (phone / tablet / PC) at runtime to decide which model size will *fit* and *run acceptably*. All numbers below are from primary sources, cited in the Sources section.\n\n## TL;DR\n\nThere is **no single \"how much RAM/VRAM do I have\" API**. You combine several coarse, privacy-capped signals into a budget:\n\n- **RAM budget** ← `navigator.deviceMemory` (capped to 0.25–8 GiB, Chromium-only) + `navigator.hardwareConcurrency` as a CPU/tier proxy.\n- **VRAM/compute budget** ← WebGPU `adapter.limits.maxBufferSize` / `maxStorageBufferBindingSize` + `shader-f16` feature.\n- **Disk budget (for caching weights)** ← `navigator.storage.estimate()` + `navigator.storage.persist()`.\n- **Live memory pressure** ← `performance.measureUserAgentSpecificMemory()` (requires cross-origin isolation), legacy `performance.memory` (Chrome-only, non-standard).\n- **Execution-path constraints** ← WebGPU-vs-WASM availability; WASM 4 GiB / memory64 / threads (SharedArrayBuffer + COOP/COEP).\n\nThen apply a safety margin (model weights + KV cache + runtime overhead must sit well under the smallest relevant budget).\n\n---\n\n## Capability-Detection Matrix\n\n| API / Signal | What it tells you | Units / values | Browser support | Caveats |\n|---|---|---|---|---|\n| `navigator.deviceMemory` | Approx. device **RAM** | GiB, one of **0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8** (rounded to nearest power of 2, ÷1024, then clamped) | Chrome 63+, Edge 79+, Opera 50+, Samsung Internet 8.2+; **Firefox NO, Safari NO** (~77% global). Secure context only | Capped low/high for anti-fingerprinting; an 8 means \"≥8\". Undefined ⇒ assume low-end |\n| `navigator.hardwareConcurrency` | **Logical** CPU cores (threads) | Integer ≥1 (e.g. 8 for a 4-core/8-thread CPU) | **Baseline, widely available** (all major browsers since ~Mar 2022) | UA may under-report; tier proxy, not a guarantee of usable parallelism |\n| `navigator.storage.estimate()` | **Disk** usable for this origin (for caching weights) | `{usage, quota}` in **bytes**; optional `usageDetails` | Baseline widely available since Sep 2023; secure context; works in workers | `quota` is conservative/imprecise; based on **total** disk size (not free space) to avoid fingerprinting |\n| `navigator.storage.persist()` | Upgrade from **best-effort → persistent** (survives eviction) | Promise→boolean | With Storage API | Best-effort default can be evicted (LRU) under pressure; Safari evicts after **7 days** of no interaction |\n| WebGPU `navigator.gpu` + `requestAdapter()` | **Is GPU compute available** | adapter or **null** | Chrome/Edge 113+ desktop, 121+ Android; Safari 26 / recent; Firefox shipping. `navigator.gpu` undefined ⇒ no WebGPU | null even when present if accel disabled / blocklisted / no GPU |\n| `adapter.limits.maxBufferSize` | Max single GPU **buffer** ≈ practical VRAM ceiling | bytes; **default min 268,435,456 (256 MiB)**; desktop often **4,294,967,296 (4 GiB)** | Per WebGPU | Reported in **fingerprint-resistant tiers**; request higher via `requiredLimits` in `requestDevice()` |\n| `adapter.limits.maxStorageBufferBindingSize` | Max **storage buffer** bound to a shader | bytes; **default min 134,217,728 (128 MiB)** | Per WebGPU | Mobile (iOS/Android) commonly stays near the 128 MiB default; often the real bottleneck for large weight tensors |\n| `adapter.features.has('shader-f16')` | **16-bit float** in shaders (halves weight footprint, faster) | boolean feature flag | WebGPU optional feature | Must be in `requiredFeatures` of `requestDevice()`; absence forces f32 (≈2× memory) |\n| `adapter.info` | GPU vendor/architecture (tiering) | `{vendor, architecture, device, description}` | WebGPU (recent) | Coarse; not reliable VRAM source |\n| `performance.measureUserAgentSpecificMemory()` | **Total page memory** (JS+DOM+workers) | `{bytes, breakdown[]}` in bytes | Chromium (Chrome 89+); \"limited availability / experimental\" | **Requires `crossOriginIsolated` (COOP+COEP) + secure context**; throws SecurityError otherwise; meant for periodic sampling |\n| `performance.memory` | JS **heap** size (legacy) | `{jsHeapSizeLimit, totalJSHeapSize, usedJSHeapSize}` bytes | **Chrome-only, non-standard, effectively deprecated** | Never on workers; superseded by the API above |\n| `crossOriginIsolated` | Whether SAB / precise memory / threads are unlocked | boolean | Widely supported | Needs `COOP: same-origin` + `COEP: require-corp` (or `credentialless`) |\n\n---\n\n## 1. `navigator.deviceMemory` — RAM signal\n\n- **Returns** a float in **GiB**: one of `0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8` (the upper bound is implementation-defined; MDN's worked example uses a 2–32 range to illustrate the mechanism).\n- **Computation:** actual RAM → round to nearest power of 2 → ÷1024 → clamp to lower/upper bounds. The clamping is explicitly to **curtail fingerprinting** and protect users of very-low/very-high-memory devices.\n- **Support:** Chromium only — **Chrome 63+, Edge 79+, Opera 50+, Samsung Internet 8.2+**. **Not implemented in Firefox or Safari** (~77% global coverage). Secure-context only. Chromium also exposes the `Sec-CH-Device-Memory` client-hint HTTP header (server opts in via `Accept-CH`).\n- **Practical use:** treat `8` as \"≥8 GiB\", treat `undefined` (Safari/Firefox) as a low/unknown tier and fall back to other signals. This is your **system-RAM budget** for WASM/CPU inference and for non-WebGPU model loading.\n\n## 2. `navigator.hardwareConcurrency` — CPU cores\n\n- **Returns** the number of **logical** processors (e.g. 8 for a 4-core/8-thread CPU), integer ≥1. The browser may report fewer than physically present, and MDN warns not to treat it as an absolute core count.\n- **Support:** **Baseline, widely available** across all major browsers. No secure-context requirement.\n- **Use:** sets the worker/thread pool size for WASM inference and serves as a rough device-tier proxy (phones report 4–8; PCs 8–32).\n\n## 3. `navigator.storage.estimate()` / `persist()` — caching model weights\n\n- **`estimate()`** → `{usage, quota}` in **bytes** (+ optional `usageDetails` per subsystem). Values are deliberately **imprecise** (compression/dedup/obfuscation). `quota` is derived from **total disk size**, not free space (anti-fingerprinting), so it is an *upper* bound, not guaranteed-free space.\n- **Covers** IndexedDB, **Cache API**, **OPFS**, and WASM code caching — the three places model weights are typically cached. Exceeding quota throws `QuotaExceededError`.\n- **Typical quotas:**\n  - **Chrome/Chromium (Edge):** up to **60% of total disk** per origin (e.g. ~600 GiB on a 1 TiB drive); browser-wide cap ~80%.\n  - **Firefox:** best-effort = smaller of **10% of disk or 10 GiB** (group limit); persistent up to **50% of disk** (cap 8 TiB).\n  - **Safari/WebKit:** browser ~**60% of disk** per origin (80% across all origins); embedded web views ~15%/20%.\n- **Eviction:** default is **best-effort** — can be evicted under storage pressure via **LRU**, and on eviction **all** of an origin's data (IndexedDB + Cache + OPFS) is wiped together. **Safari proactively deletes** origins with **no user interaction for 7 days**.\n- **`navigator.storage.persist()`** upgrades the origin to **persistent** (only user-initiated deletion), which you want for multi-GB model caches you don't want re-downloaded.\n\n## 4. WebGPU — VRAM / compute budget\n\n- **Detect availability:** `if (!navigator.gpu) → no WebGPU`. Then `const adapter = await navigator.gpu.requestAdapter()` — **`adapter` can be null** even when `navigator.gpu` exists (accel disabled, GPU blocklisted, no GPU, repeated GPU-process crashes). Only after a successful `requestDevice()` can you run compute.\n- **Guaranteed-minimum (default) limits** (adapters may exceed; from MDN `GPUSupportedLimits`):\n  - `maxBufferSize` = **268,435,456 bytes (256 MiB)**\n  - `maxStorageBufferBindingSize` = **134,217,728 bytes (128 MiB)**\n  - `maxUniformBufferBindingSize` = 65,536 (64 KiB); `maxComputeWorkgroupStorageSize` = 16,384 (16 KiB)\n- **Mobile vs desktop reality:**\n  - **Desktop** adapters commonly support `maxBufferSize` up to **4,294,967,296 bytes (4 GiB)**, but you must opt in by passing `requiredLimits: { maxBufferSize: ... }` to `requestDevice()` — otherwise you get the **256 MiB default**.\n  - **Mobile** (iOS Safari / Android Chrome) frequently can't go beyond the **256 MiB buffer / 128 MiB storage-binding** defaults. This caps the largest single weight tensor / KV buffer you can bind, which is often the *real* limiter for model size on phones — not total RAM.\n  - Browsers report limits in **fingerprint-resistant tiers** (e.g. 2048/8192/32768), so a device with a true 16384 may report 8192.\n- **f16:** `adapter.features.has('shader-f16')` (request via `requiredFeatures: ['shader-f16']`). f16 ≈ **halves** weight memory and bandwidth vs f32; absence forces f32 and roughly doubles the footprint.\n- **Fallback to WASM:** if `navigator.gpu`/adapter/device is unavailable, fall back to CPU WASM inference (ONNX Runtime Web / wasm backend). The performance gap is large — reports cite **10–15×** WebGPU-vs-WASM differences depending on model/hardware.\n\n## 5. Live memory / pressure\n\n- **`performance.measureUserAgentSpecificMemory()`** → `{bytes, breakdown[]}` estimating **whole-page** memory (window + iframes + workers, DOM + JS). **Hard requirement: `crossOriginIsolated === true`** (COOP+COEP) **and** a secure context, else it throws `SecurityError`. Designed for **periodic, randomized-interval** sampling, not one-shot. Chrome 89+, \"experimental/limited.\"\n- **`performance.memory`** (legacy) exposes JS-heap figures (`jsHeapSizeLimit`, `totalJSHeapSize`, `usedJSHeapSize`) but is **Chrome-only, non-standard, effectively deprecated**, and unavailable in workers. Use only as a Chrome-only heuristic.\n\n## 6. WASM execution constraints\n\n- **32-bit address space:** standard wasm linear memory is 32-bit → **hard 4 GiB cap** (page size 64 KiB). A single-memory wasm module historically can't address more than ~4 GiB, which bounds CPU-side model size.\n- **memory64:** raises addressing to 64-bit, removing the 4 GiB ceiling. **Reached Phase 4 / merged into the spec**; shipping in **Chrome and Firefox**, **not yet in Safari** (as of 2025). Part of \"WebAssembly 3.0.\"\n- **Threads:** WASM threads need **`SharedArrayBuffer` + atomics**, which require **cross-origin isolation** — serve **`Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy: same-origin`** and **`Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy: require-corp`** (or `credentialless`); verify with `self.crossOriginIsolated === true`. Isolation requirement dates to the Spectre mitigations (Chrome M92). **SIMD** is broadly shipped (out from behind the flag in Firefox; still flagged in Safari as of 2025) and matters a lot for CPU inference throughput.\n\n## 7. Practical heuristics (WebLLM / transformers.js)\n\n- **WebLLM** drives fit decisions from **per-model config fields** in its model records (`src/config.ts`):\n  - **`vram_required_MB`** — explicit VRAM estimate per model (examples range from ~238 MB for an embedding model to ~31,153 MB for large LLMs). Compare against the WebGPU budget.\n  - **`low_resource_required`** — boolean; `true` for small models (e.g. Llama-3.2-1B) that run on phones.\n  - **`required_features`** — e.g. `[\"shader-f16\"]`; the runtime checks `adapter.features` and skips models the device can't support.\n  - **`context_window_size`** overrides (1024 / 2048 / 4096) — smaller context cuts **KV-cache** memory, a major lever on constrained devices.\n  - `cacheBackend: \"cache\"` ⇒ weights cached via Cache API (subject to the storage quotas/eviction above).\n- **transformers.js** runs ONNX Runtime Web with a **WebGPU backend** (falls back to **WASM/CPU**), and leans on **quantization** to fit: f32 = 4 B/param, **q8 = 1 B/param (≈4× smaller)**, **q4 = 0.5 B/param (≈8× smaller)**. So a 1B model is ~4 GB (f32), ~1 GB (q8), ~0.5 GB (q4).\n- **Budgeting rule of thumb that follows from the above:**\n  - **VRAM budget** ≈ `adapter.limits.maxBufferSize` (and watch `maxStorageBufferBindingSize` for the largest single tensor) — on mobile assume ~256 MiB / 128 MiB unless the adapter reports more.\n  - **RAM budget** ≈ `navigator.deviceMemory` GiB (undefined ⇒ treat as low tier).\n  - **Total need** = quantized weights + KV cache (grows with context × layers) + runtime/working overhead.\n  - **Safety margin:** keep the model comfortably under the budget (commonly target ~50–70% of the available budget) because deviceMemory/limits are coarse and shared with the rest of the page and OS; leave headroom for KV-cache growth, the browser, and other tabs.\n- **General sizing reality cited by practitioners:** quantized models **<2 GB** run at interactive speeds on consumer hardware; **≤2B-param** models are the comfortable interactive ceiling on mainstream/mobile devices; ~3.8B (e.g. Phi-3-mini) wants roughly **4–8 GB** GPU memory at f16. Desktop WebGPU adapters typically expose ~1–4 GB usable; mobile much less.\n\n---\n\n## Recommended detection flow (synthesis)\n\n1. **Secure context + COOP/COEP** so `crossOriginIsolated` is true (unlocks SAB/WASM threads + precise memory).\n2. `navigator.gpu?` → `requestAdapter()` → check **null**; read `adapter.limits.maxBufferSize`, `maxStorageBufferBindingSize`, `adapter.features.has('shader-f16')`, `adapter.info`. This is the **VRAM/compute budget**.\n3. Read `navigator.deviceMemory` (RAM budget) and `navigator.hardwareConcurrency` (thread pool / tier). Handle `undefined` on Safari/Firefox as low/unknown.\n4. `navigator.storage.estimate()` to confirm the quantized weights fit on disk; call `navigator.storage.persist()` for large caches.\n5. Optionally sample `performance.measureUserAgentSpecificMemory()` periodically to back off if the page is near pressure.\n6. Pick model + quantization + context so (weights + KV + overhead) sits under the **smallest** relevant budget with margin; **fall back WebGPU → WASM** (and prefer SIMD + threads) when no usable adapter.\n\n---\n\n## Sources\n\n- navigator.deviceMemory — MDN: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Navigator/deviceMemory\n- Device Memory support — caniuse: https://caniuse.com/mdn-api_navigator_devicememory\n- Device Memory API — Chrome for Developers: https://developer.chrome.com/blog/device-memory\n- Device Memory spec — W3C/GitHub: https://github.com/w3c/device-memory\n- navigator.hardwareConcurrency — MDN: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Navigator/hardwareConcurrency\n- StorageManager.estimate() — MDN: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/StorageManager/estimate\n- Storage quotas and eviction criteria — MDN: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Storage_API/Storage_quotas_and_eviction_criteria\n- Storage API overview — MDN: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Storage_API\n- Estimating available storage space — Chrome for Developers: https://developer.chrome.com/blog/estimating-available-storage-space\n- Updates to Storage Policy — WebKit: https://webkit.org/blog/14403/updates-to-storage-policy/\n- GPUSupportedLimits (default limit values) — MDN: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/GPUSupportedLimits\n- GPUDevice.limits — https://www.sitepoint.com/webgpu-vs-webasm-transformers-js/\n- Running AI models locally in the browser (WebGPU & WASM) — Mad Devs: https://maddevs.io/writeups/running-ai-models-locally-in-the-browser/\n\n### Notes on confidence / contested points\n- **deviceMemory upper bound:** MDN's `8` (and the worked 2–32 example) reflect implementation-defined clamps; the exact cap can change per browser version. Treat the top value as \"≥\".\n- **WebGPU limit values are tiered** for fingerprinting resistance, so `adapter.limits` reflect bucketed values, not the GPU's true maximum — budget conservatively.\n- **memory64 / WASM 3.0 \"all major browsers\"** claims come from secondary blogs; the verified, conservative statement is: memory64 reached Phase 4 / merged, ships in Chrome and Firefox, **not yet in Safari**."

absence forces f32 (≈2× memory) |\n| `adapter.info` | GPU vendor/architecture (tiering) | `{vendor, architecture, device, description}` | WebGPU (recent) | Coarse; not reliable VRAM source |\n| `performance.measureUserAgentSpecificMemory()` | **Total page memory** (JS+DOM+workers) | `{bytes, breakdown[]}` in bytes | Chromium (Chrome 89+); \"limited availability / experimental\" | **Requires `crossOriginIsolated` (COOP+COEP) + secure context**; throws SecurityError otherwise; meant for periodic sampling |\n| `performance.memory` | JS **heap** size (legacy) | `{jsHeapSizeLimit, totalJSHeapSize, usedJSHeapSize}` bytes | **Chrome-only, non-standard, effectively deprecated** | Never on workers; superseded by the API above |\n| `crossOriginIsolated` | Whether SAB / precise memory / threads are unlocked | boolean | Widely supported | Needs `COOP: same-origin` + `COEP: require-corp` (or `credentialless`) |\n\n---\n\n## 1. `navigator.deviceMemory` — RAM signal\n\n- **Returns** a float in **GiB**: one of `0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8` (the upper bound is implementation-defined; MDN's worked example uses a 2–32 range to illustrate the mechanism).\n- **Computation:** actual RAM → round to nearest power of 2 → ÷1024 → clamp to lower/upper bounds. The clamping is explicitly to **curtail fingerprinting** and protect users of very-low/very-high-memory devices.\n- **Support:** Chromium only — **Chrome 63+, Edge 79+, Opera 50+, Samsung Internet 8.2+**. **Not implemented in Firefox or Safari** (~77% global coverage). Secure-context only. Chromium also exposes the `Sec-CH-Device-Memory` client-hint HTTP header (server opts in via `Accept-CH`).\n- **Practical use:** treat `8` as \"≥8 GiB\", treat `undefined` (Safari/Firefox) as a low/unknown tier and fall back to other signals. This is your **system-RAM budget** for WASM/CPU inference and for non-WebGPU model loading.\n\n## 2. `navigator.hardwareConcurrency` — CPU cores\n\n- **Returns** the number of **logical** processors (e.g. 8 for a 4-core/8-thread CPU), integer ≥1. The browser may report fewer than physically present, and MDN warns not to treat it as an absolute core count.\n- **Support:** **Baseline, widely available** across all major browsers. No secure-context requirement.\n- **Use:** sets the worker/thread pool size for WASM inference and serves as a rough device-tier proxy (phones report 4–8; PCs 8–32).\n\n## 3. `navigator.storage.estimate()` / `persist()` — caching model weights\n\n- **`estimate()`** → `{usage, quota}` in **bytes** (+ optional `usageDetails` per subsystem). Values are deliberately **imprecise** (compression/dedup/obfuscation). `quota` is derived from **total disk size**, not free space (anti-fingerprinting), so it is an *upper* bound, not guaranteed-free space.\n- **Covers** IndexedDB, **Cache API**, **OPFS**, and WASM code caching — the three places model weights are typically cached. Exceeding quota throws `QuotaExceededError`.\n- **Typical quotas:**\n  - **Chrome/Chromium (Edge):** up to **60% of total disk** per origin (e.g. ~600 GiB on a 1 TiB drive); browser-wide cap ~80%.\n  - **Firefox:** best-effort = smaller of **10% of disk or 10 GiB** (group limit); persistent up to **50% of disk** (cap 8 TiB).\n  - **Safari/WebKit:** browser ~**60% of disk** per origin (80% across all origins); embedded web views ~15%/20%.\n- **Eviction:** default is **best-effort** — can be evicted under storage pressure via **LRU**, and on eviction **all** of an origin's data (IndexedDB + Cache + OPFS) is wiped together. **Safari proactively deletes** origins with **no user interaction for 7 days**.\n- **`navigator.storage.persist()`** upgrades the origin to **persistent** (only user-initiated deletion), which you want for multi-GB model caches you don't want re-downloaded.\n\n## 4. WebGPU — VRAM / compute budget\n\n- **Detect availability:** `if (!navigator.gpu) → no WebGPU`. Then `const adapter = await navigator.gpu.requestAdapter()` — **`adapter` can be null** even when `navigator.gpu` exists (accel disabled, GPU blocklisted, no GPU, repeated GPU-process crashes). Only after a successful `requestDevice()` can you run compute.\n- **Guaranteed-minimum (default) limits** (adapters may exceed; from MDN `GPUSupportedLimits`):\n  - `maxBufferSize` = **268,435,456 bytes (256 MiB)**\n  - `maxStorageBufferBindingSize` = **134,217,728 bytes (128 MiB)**\n  - `maxUniformBufferBindingSize` = 65,536 (64 KiB); `maxComputeWorkgroupStorageSize` = 16,384 (16 KiB)\n- **Mobile vs desktop reality:**\n  - **Desktop** adapters commonly support `maxBufferSize` up to **4,294,967,296 bytes (4 GiB)**, but you must opt in by passing `requiredLimits: { maxBufferSize: ... }` to `requestDevice()` — otherwise you get the **256 MiB default**.\n  - **Mobile** (iOS Safari / Android Chrome) frequently can't go beyond the **256 MiB buffer / 128 MiB storage-binding** defaults. This caps the largest single weight tensor / KV buffer you can bind, which is often the *real* limiter for model size on phones — not total RAM.\n  - Browsers report limits in **fingerprint-resistant tiers** (e.g. 2048/8192/32768), so a device with a true 16384 may report 8192.\n- **f16:** `adapter.features.has('shader-f16')` (request via `requiredFeatures: ['shader-f16']`). f16 ≈ **halves** weight memory and bandwidth vs f32; absence forces f32 and roughly doubles the footprint.\n- **Fallback to WASM:** if `navigator.gpu`/adapter/device is unavailable, fall back to CPU WASM inference (ONNX Runtime Web / wasm backend). The performance gap is large — reports cite **10–15×** WebGPU-vs-WASM differences depending on model/hardware.\n\n## 5. Live memory / pressure\n\n- **`performance.measureUserAgentSpecificMemory()`** → `{bytes, breakdown[]}` estimating **whole-page** memory (window + iframes + workers, DOM + JS). **Hard requirement: `crossOriginIsolated === true`** (COOP+COEP) **and** a secure context, else it throws `SecurityError`. Designed for **periodic, randomized-interval** sampling, not one-shot. Chrome 89+, \"experimental/limited.\"\n- **`performance.memory`** (legacy) exposes JS-heap figures (`jsHeapSizeLimit`, `totalJSHeapSize`, `usedJSHeapSize`) but is **Chrome-only, non-standard, effectively deprecated**, and unavailable in workers. Use only as a Chrome-only heuristic.\n\n## 6. WASM execution constraints\n\n- **32-bit address space:** standard wasm linear memory is 32-bit → **hard 4 GiB cap** (page size 64 KiB). A single-memory wasm module historically can't address more than ~4 GiB, which bounds CPU-side model size.\n- **memory64:** raises addressing to 64-bit, removing the 4 GiB ceiling. **Reached Phase 4 / merged into the spec**; shipping in **Chrome and Firefox**, **not yet in Safari** (as of 2025). Part of \"WebAssembly 3.0.\"\n- **Threads:** WASM threads need **`SharedArrayBuffer` + atomics**, which require **cross-origin isolation** — serve **`Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy: same-origin`** and **`Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy: require-corp`** (or `credentialless`); verify with `self.crossOriginIsolated === true`. Isolation requirement dates to the Spectre mitigations (Chrome M92). **SIMD** is broadly shipped (out from behind the flag in Firefox; still flagged in Safari as of 2025) and matters a lot for CPU inference throughput.\n\n## 7. Practical heuristics (WebLLM / transformers.js)\n\n- **WebLLM** drives fit decisions from **per-model config fields** in its model records (`src/config.ts`):\n  - **`vram_required_MB`** — explicit VRAM estimate per model (examples range from ~238 MB for an embedding model to ~31,153 MB for large LLMs). Compare against the WebGPU budget.\n  - **`low_resource_required`** — boolean; `true` for small models (e.g. Llama-3.2-1B) that run on phones.\n  - **`required_features`** — e.g. `[\"shader-f16\"]`; the runtime checks `adapter.features` and skips models the device can't support.\n  - **`context_window_size`** overrides (1024 / 2048 / 4096) — smaller context cuts **KV-cache** memory, a major lever on constrained devices.\n  - `cacheBackend: \"cache\"` ⇒ weights cached via Cache API (subject to the storage quotas/eviction above).\n- **transformers.js** runs ONNX Runtime Web with a **WebGPU backend** (falls back to **WASM/CPU**), and leans on **quantization** to fit: f32 = 4 B/param, **q8 = 1 B/param (≈4× smaller)**, **q4 = 0.5 B/param (≈8× smaller)**. So a 1B model is ~4 GB (f32), ~1 GB (q8), ~0.5 GB (q4).\n- **Budgeting rule of thumb that follows from the above:**\n  - **VRAM budget** ≈ `adapter.limits.maxBufferSize` (and watch `maxStorageBufferBindingSize` for the largest single tensor) — on mobile assume ~256 MiB / 128 MiB unless the adapter reports more.\n  - **RAM budget** ≈ `navigator.deviceMemory` GiB (undefined ⇒ treat as low tier).\n  - **Total need** = quantized weights + KV cache (grows with context × layers) + runtime/working overhead.\n  - **Safety margin:** keep the model comfortably under the budget (commonly target ~50–70% of the available budget) because deviceMemory/limits are coarse and shared with the rest of the page and OS; leave headroom for KV-cache growth, the browser, and other tabs.\n- **General sizing reality cited by practitioners:** quantized models **<2 GB** run at interactive speeds on consumer hardware; **≤2B-param** models are the comfortable interactive ceiling on mainstream/mobile devices; ~3.8B (e.g. Phi-3-mini) wants roughly **4–8 GB** GPU memory at f16. Desktop WebGPU adapters typically expose ~1–4 GB usable; mobile much less.\n\n---\n\n## Recommended detection flow (synthesis)\n\n1. **Secure context + COOP/COEP** so `crossOriginIsolated` is true (unlocks SAB/WASM threads + precise memory).\n2. `navigator.gpu?` → `requestAdapter()` → check **null**; read `adapter.limits.maxBufferSize`, `maxStorageBufferBindingSize`, `adapter.features.has('shader-f16')`, `adapter.info`. This is the **VRAM/compute budget**.\n3. Read `navigator.deviceMemory` (RAM budget) and `navigator.hardwareConcurrency` (thread pool / tier). Handle `undefined` on Safari/Firefox as low/unknown.\n4. `navigator.storage.estimate()` to confirm the quantized weights fit on disk; call `navigator.storage.persist()` for large caches.\n5. Optionally sample `performance.measureUserAgentSpecificMemory()` periodically to back off if the page is near pressure.\n6. Pick model + quantization + context so (weights + KV + overhead) sits under the **smallest** relevant budget with margin; **fall back WebGPU → WASM** (and prefer SIMD + threads) when no usable adapter.\n\n---\n\n## Sources\n\n- navigator.deviceMemory — MDN: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Navigator/deviceMemory\n- Device Memory support — caniuse: implementation-defined clamps; the exact cap can change per browser version. Treat the top value as \"≥\".\n- **WebGPU limit values are tiered** for fingerprinting resistance, so `adapter.limits` reflect bucketed values, not the GPU's true maximum — budget conservatively.\n- **memory64 / WASM 3.0 \"all major browsers\"** claims come from secondary blogs; the verified, conservative statement is: memory64 reached Phase 4 / merged, ships in Chrome and Firefox, **not yet in Safari**."

from worker.ts\n    const worker = new Worker(new URL('./worker.ts', import.meta.url), {\n      type: 'module',\n    });\n\n    worker.onmessage = (event: MessageEvent<WorkerMessage>) => {\n initialized\n          if (payload === 'Worker initialized' && !autoLoadTriggeredRef.current) {\n            autoLoadTriggeredRef.current = true;\n            // Use setTimeout to ensure state is updated before loading\n            setTimeout(() => {\n              if (workerRef.current) {\n                setStatus('loading');\n                setStatusText('Starting automatic [2026-06-09T22:53:11.815Z] [INFO]         "content": "name:\tlink-assistant/formal-ai\ndescription:\t100% symbolic, logical, and data driven formal language processor\n--\n# formal-ai\n\nFormal AI is a Rust implementation of a symbolic, deterministic assistant that exposes OpenAI-shaped interfaces without neural-network inference.\n\nThe current implementation covers the surface area requested in issue #1:\n\n- library API for symbolic prompt handling\n- CLI chat command\n- HTTP API server with `/v1/chat/completions` and `/v1/responses`\n- Telegram bot CLI with long polling by default and an opt-in webhook server, configured through [`lino-arguments`](https://github.com/link-foundation/lino-arguments)\n- human-readable Links Notation knowledge and dataset export through `lino-objects-codec`\n- Docker-in-Docker Telegram bot image based on `konard/box-dind:2.1.1`\n- GitHub Pages markdown chat demo backed by a Rust-generated WebAssembly worker\n- Electron desktop shell that starts the local Rust HTTP API and reuses the web chat\n- VS Code extension (desktop **and** web/`vscode.dev`) that embeds the same chat in a Webview around the same HTTP/web boundary\n\nProject direction is tracked in [VISION.md](VISION.md), [GOALS.md](GOALS.md), and [NON-GOALS.md](NON-GOALS.md). Implementation progress against the vision is tracked in [ROADMAP.md](ROADMAP.md). The issue #12 synthesis is in [docs/case-studies/issue-12/README.md](docs/case-studies/issue-12/README.md).\n\n## Universal Problem-Solving Algorithm\n\nEvery prompt — a greeting, a math question, a translation, a \"write a program\"\nrequest, an agent action, or something the assistant has never seen — walks the\nsame loop. The skeleton is **decompose the problem into tasks, derive a test\n(experiment) for each task, draft candidate solutions, select the smallest\nsufficient draft, and compose the drafts back into one solution**:\n\n![Universal Problem Solving Algorithm: a problem fans out into tasks, each task into tests, each test into candidate drafts, and the selected drafts compose back into a single solution](docs/assets/universal-problem-solving-algorithm.jpg)\n\nThis is not a separate subsystem bolted onto a catalogue of intents — it is the\nmain solver path. `src/solver.rs::UniversalSolver` runs the same 11-step loop\nfor every impulse:\n\n| Diagram stage | Loop steps in `src/solver.rs` |\n| --- | --- |\n| `Problem` | 1. Impulse, 2. Formalization (Links-Notation intent), 3. Context, 4. History lookup |\n| `decomposition → tasks` | 5. Decomposition into sub-impulses (`record_decomposition`) |\n| `experiment → tests` | 6. TDD-style validation (`record_validation`) |\n| `selection → drafts` | 7. Solution synthesis (`record_candidates`), 8. Combination of the smallest sufficient candidate |\n| `composition → solutions → Solution` | 9. Verification, 10. Simplification, 11. Documentation with a `trace:` pointer |\n\nEvery step appends its own event to the append-only log, so the chat can answer\n\"why did you do that?\" from the recorded experience. The full algorithm,\nincluding the reasoning-under-unknowns path (`src/solver_unknown_reasoning.rs`)\nand intent formalization (`src/intent_formalization.rs`), is documented in\n[VISION.md](VISION.md#universal-problem-solving-algorithm) and\n[ARCHITECTURE.md](ARCHITECTURE.md). How much of the vision is built versus still\nplanned — including the open industry-benchmark coverage gap — is tracked in",

[2026-06-09T22:53:56.909Z] [INFO]         "content": "=== preferences.js ===\n// Tiny Links-Notation reader/writer for the demo's user preferences.\n//\n// The serialized shape mirrors the indented format used elsewhere in the\n// repository (see `data/seed/*.lino`) so the same format that grounds the\n// solver's knowledge also grounds the demo's UI state. Keeping the storage\n// representation in Links Notation makes the persisted state portable and\n// human-readable in DevTools.\n\n(function (global) {\n  \"use strict\";\n\n  var STORAGE_KEY = \"formal-ai.preferences.v1\";\n  var ROOT = \"demo_preferences\";\n\n  function escapeValue(value) {\n    return String(value).replace(/\\\\/g, \"\\\\\\\\\").replace(/\"/g, '\\\\\"');\n  }\n\n  function unescapeValue(value) {\n    return value.replace(/\\\\\"/g, '\"').replace(/\\\\\\\\/g, \"\\\\\");\n  }\n\n  function formatLinksNotation(record) {\n    var lines = [ROOT];\n    var keys = Object.keys(record);\n    for (var index = 0; index < keys.length; index += 1) {\n      var key = keys[index];\n      var raw = record[key];\n      if (raw === undefined || raw === null) {\n        continue;\n      }\n      var value = typeof raw === \"boolean\" ? (raw ? \"on\" : \"off\") : raw;\n      lines.push(\"  \" + key + ' \"' + escapeValue(value) + '\"');\n    }\n    return lines.join(\"\\n\");\n  }\n\n  function parseLinksNotation(text) {\n    if (typeof text !== \"string\" || !text.trim()) {\n      return null;\n    }\n    var lines = text.split(/\\r?\\n/);\n    if (lines.length === 0) {\n      return null;\n    }\n    var header = lines.shift();\n    if (!header || header.trim() !== ROOT) {\n      return null;\n    }\n    var record = {};\n    var entryPattern = /^\\s+([a-zA-Z0-9_]+)\\s+\"((?:[^\"\\\\]|\\\\.)*)\"\\s*$/;\n    for (var index = 0; index < lines.length; index += 1) {\n      var rawLine = lines[index];\n      if (!rawLine.trim()) {\n        continue;\n      }\n      var match = entryPattern.exec(rawLine);\n      if (!match) {\n        continue;\n      }\n      var key = match[1];\n      var value = unescapeValue(match[2]);\n      if (value === \"on\" || value === \"off\") {\n        record[key] = value === \"on\";\n      } else {\n        record[key] = value;\n      }\n    }\n    return record;\n  }\n\n  function getStorage() {\n    try {\n      if (typeof global.localStorage === \"undefined\") {\n        return null;\n      }\n      return global.localStorage;\n    } catch (_error) {\n      return null;\n    }\n  }\n\n  function loadPreferences(defaults) {\n    var safeDefaults = defaults && typeof defaults === \"object\" ? defaults : {};\n    var storage = getStorage();\n    if (!storage) {\n      return Object.assign({}, safeDefaults);\n    }\n    try {\n      var raw = storage.getItem(STORAGE_KEY);\n      var parsed = parseLinksNotation(raw);\n      if (!parsed) {\n        return Object.assign({}, safeDefaults);\n      }\n      return Object.assign({}, safeDefaults, parsed);\n    } catch (_error) {\n      return Object.assign({}, safeDefaults);\n    }\n  }\n\n  function savePreferences(values) {\n    var storage = getStorage();\n    if (!storage) {\n      return null;\n    }\n    try {\n      var serialized = formatLinksNotation(values);\n      storage.setItem(STORAGE_KEY, serialized);\n      return serialized;\n    } catch (_error) {\n      return null;\n    }\n  }\n\n  global.FormalAiPreferences = {\n    load: loadPreferences,\n    save: savePreferences,\n    format: formatLinksNotation,\n    parse: parseLinksNotation,\n    STORAGE_KEY: STORAGE_KEY,\n    ROOT: ROOT,\n  };\n})(typeof window !== \"undefined\" ? window : globalThis);",

Here is the structured summary.\n\n---\n\n# formal-ai — UI Best Practices & Reusable Components (for `model-in-browser`)\n\n## Repository status\nAccessible and public. **License: The Unlicense** (public domain — code can be copied/adapted with zero attribution or licensing constraints). Primary language Rust, but ~3.2 MB of JavaScript + HTML/CSS power a browser chat demo, an Electron desktop shell, and a VS Code extension. Description: \"100% symbolic, logical, and data driven formal language processor.\" No GitHub topics set.\n\nImportant caveat for our purposes: formal-ai runs a **symbolic, deterministic** engine compiled to WebAssembly — it does **not** run neural networks / LLM inference in the browser. So there is **no model-download, no WebGPU/WASM-model loader, no token-streaming, and no model-selection UI to copy**. What it does offer is a mature, dependency-light **chat + markdown + theming + capability-detection + GitHub-release-download** UI layer that is directly reusable for a model-in-browser project's shell.\n\n## Frontend stack\n- **React 18.3** (`react`, `react-dom`) — but authored with `React.createElement` (aliased `h`), **no JSX, no TSX**. Plain `.js`, ES modules.\n- **Bundler: Bun** (`bun build … --target browser --format iife --minify`) producing a committed `vendor.bundle.js`. No Vite/Webpack. Not TypeScript.\n- **Markdown:** `marked` 17 + **`dompurify`** 3.3 for sanitization.\n- **i18n:** `lino-i18n` (custom Links-Notation catalogs), 4 languages (en/ru/zh/hi).\n- **OCR (optional):** `tesseract.js` 7 (separate `ocr.bundle.js`).\n- **Engine:** Rust→WASM worker (`src/web/wasm-worker/src/lib.rs` → generated `formal_ai_worker.js` glue + `formal_ai_worker.wasm`, 1.77 MB; the glue file is a build artifact, not hand-written).\n- **Shells:** Electron (`desktop/`, electron-builder, multi-OS releases) and a VS Code webview extension (`vscode/`, desktop + `vscode.dev` web host).\n\nApp is a single `<div id=\"root\">` (`src/web/index.html`) with plain `<script>` tags loaded in order: vendor bundle → `seed_loader.js` → `preferences.js` → `i18n.js` → `syntax-highlight.js` → `memory.js` → `app.js`. Asset cache-busting via `?v=__FORMAL_AI_ASSET_VERSION__` placeholders.\n\n## Markdown rendering approach\nIn `src/web/app.js`:\n- `markdownHtml(value)` → `marked.parse(text, { breaks: true, gfm: true })` → `DOMPurify.sanitize(html)` → returned as `{ __html }` for React `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` into a `.markdown-body` div.\n- `enhanceMarkdownLinks(html)` post-processes anchors to add `target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener noreferrer\"`, an `.external-link` class and an external-link icon span (via a detached `<template>`, so it's XSS-safe).\n- **Graceful fallback** when marked/DOMPurify aren't loaded: HTML-escape + `\\n`→`<br>`.\n- `enhanceCodeBlocks(root, t)` runs after render: wraps each `<pre><code class=\"language-x\">` in a `.code-block` shell with a language label and a per-block **copy button**; idempotent so it's safe on every effect pass. There is also a whole-message \"copy markdown\" button.\n- **Syntax highlighting** is a custom dependency-free tokenizer in `src/web/syntax-highlight.js` (`window.FormalAiHighlight.highlight(code, lang)`), emitting `hljs-*` class names (drop-in compatible with highlight.js CSS themes) for rust/python/js/ts/go/c/cpp/java/csharp/ruby/bash/json/css. Everything is HTML-escaped before DOM insertion.\n- Clipboard helper `copyTextToClipboard` prefers async Clipboard API with a hidden-textarea + `execCommand` fallback for `file://`/Playwright contexts.\n\n## Theme (dark/light/auto)\n- Preference is `auto | light | dark`. `currentColorScheme()` resolves `auto` via `window.matchMedia('(prefers-color-scheme: dark)')`.\n- Applied by setting `document.documentElement` `data-theme=\"dark|light\"` (or removing it for auto); a `matchMedia('change')` listener re-renders on system theme change. CSS keys off `:root[data-theme=...]` plus CSS variables (also sets `--formal-ai-viewport-*` vars from `visualViewport` for mobile).\n- Theme + locale are even controllable by natural-language chat commands (\"dark mode\", \"тема\", \"主题\").\n\n## Persistence pattern\n`src/web/preferences.js` (`window.FormalAiPreferences`) stores all UI state in `localStorage` under `formal-ai.preferences.v1`, serialized as **Links Notation** (human-readable, booleans as `on`/`off`). `load/save/parse/format`. Shared between the chat app and the download page.\n\n## Worker request/response pattern (adaptable to an inference worker)\nIn `app.js`: `new Worker(withAssetVersion(\"formal_ai_worker.js\"))`; worker posts a `{ kind: \"ready\", mode }` handshake → app stores `workerState` (e.g. `\"wasm\"`). Requests use a `pendingResponses` Map keyed by a generated `requestId`; `requestAnswer(text, history)` posts `{ prompt, requestId, history, prefs, userContext }` and resolves the matching Promise on the reply. Includes a **desktop-server fallback** (`POST /v1/chat/completions`) and an in-process `localFallbackAnswer`. Note: responses are **non-streaming** (`stream: false`); there is no token-streaming UI. \"Thinking steps\" are rendered as a static `<ol>` after the fact, not streamed.\n\n## Capability / platform messaging — the most directly relevant piece\n`src/web/download/` is a self-contained, **dependency-free** download landing page (ported from `konard/vk-bot-desktop`) that is the closest analog to a \"model picker / model download\" screen:\n- `detectOperatingSystem()` uses `navigator.userAgentData.platform` → `navigator.platform` → UA string to pick `macos|windows|linux|unknown`; overridable via `?os=` query or `window.__FORMAL_AI_DOWNLOAD_OS__` (great for testing). `detectLocaleFromBrowser()` does the same for language.\n- `downloadOptions` is a declarative catalog of build variants (os, arch, asset prefix, extension); `downloadFamilies()` groups them into primary + secondary options per OS.\n- Resolves real assets from the **GitHub Releases API** (`api.github.com/repos/link-assistant/formal-ai/releases/latest`), with legacy-name fallbacks, checksum, and provenance links.\n- Localized copy for en/ru/zh/hi; shares the same `data-theme` + `FormalAiPreferences` system as the chat. Has light/dark app-preview screenshots per locale.\n\nThis OS/arch detection + declarative variant catalog + \"recommended for your device\" + GitHub-release resolution is exactly the shape a model-in-browser app needs for \"detect your device → recommend a model build/quantization → download,\" just swapping OS variants for model/quantization variants and capability checks for WebGPU/WASM/threads/RAM probes (which formal-ai does **not** implement).\n\n## Components worth copying into `model-in-browser`\nPaths are in `github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai`:\n\n1. **`src/web/preferences.js`** — tiny localStorage preference store (load/save/parse/format). Copy nearly as-is; swap the serialization for JSON if Links Notation isn't wanted.\n2. **`src/web/syntax-highlight.js`** — dependency-free, `hljs-*`-compatible code highlighter. Excellent for rendering model-generated code without a heavy dep.\n3. **`markdownHtml` + `enhanceMarkdownLinks` + `enhanceCodeBlocks` + `copyTextToClipboard` + `flashCopied`** from **`src/web/app.js`** (around lines 2014–2200, 4297–4360) — the marked+DOMPurify pipeline, code-block wrapper with language label + copy button, and the `.markdown-body` message renderer. This is the single most reusable cluster for displaying streamed/finished LLM output (add streaming on top).\n4. **Theme effect + `currentColorScheme`/`matchMedia` listener** from `src/web/app.js` (lines ~1400–1409, 4837–4847, 4945–4960) — the `data-theme` + auto/system pattern and viewport CSS-var wiring.\n5. **`src/web/download/download.js` (+ `download.css`, `index.html`)** — device/OS detection, declarative build-variant catalog, GitHub-release asset resolution, localized + themed download UI. Adapt OS-variant logic into model/quantization-variant selection; add WebGPU/WASM capability probes where it currently keys off OS only.\n6. **Worker request/response harness** (`app.js` ~5573–5775) — `requestId`/`pendingResponses` Map pattern and the `ready` handshake; a clean template for an inference Web Worker (extend with streaming `onmessage` chunks).\n7. **`src/web/i18n.js`** — only if multi-language UI is needed; depends on `lino-i18n`.\n8. **Shells (reference, not copy-paste):** `desktop/` (electron-builder multi-OS config) and `vscode/src/lib/webview-html.cjs` / `chat-view.cjs` if a desktop or VS Code wrapper is later wanted.\n\nWhat you'll have to build yourself (absent here): WebGPU/WebAssembly-SIMD/threads/RAM capability detection, model download-progress bars, model-cache (OPFS/Cache API) management, model-selection dropdowns, and streaming-token display.\n\n## Sources\n- `gh repo view link-assistant/formal-ai` (description, README, languages, license metadata)\n- `gh api repos/link-assistant/formal-ai/git/trees/HEAD?recursive=1` (file tree)\n- File contents via `gh api …/contents/PATH | base64 -d`: `package.json`, `desktop/package.json`, `vscode/package.json`, `src/web/index.html`, `src/web/vendor-entry.js`, `src/web/app.js`, `src/web/preferences.js`, `src/web/syntax-highlight.js`, `src/web/i18n.js`, `src/web/download/download.js`, `LICENSE`\n- Default branch `main`; `formal_ai_worker.js` (1.77 MB generated WASM glue) not readable via the contents API and not a hand-written reusable component."

changelog.d/README.md ---\n# Changelog Fragments\n\nThis directory contains changelog fragments that will be collected into `CHANGELOG.md` during releases.\n\n## How to Add a Changelog Fragment\n\nWhen making changes that should be documented in the changelog, create a fragment file:\n\n```bash\n# Create a new fragment with timestamp\ntouch changelog.d/$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S)_description.md\n\n# Or manually create a file matching the pattern: YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS_description.md\n```\n\n## Fragment Format\n\nEach fragment should include a **frontmatter section** specifying the version bump type:\n\n```markdown\n---\nbump: patch\n---\n\n### Fixed\n- Description of bug fix\n```\n\n### Bump Types\n\nUse semantic versioning bump types in the frontmatter:\n\n- **`major`**: Breaking changes (incompatible API changes)\n- **`minor`**: New features (backward compatible)\n- **`patch`**: Bug fixes (backward compatible)\n\n### Content Categories\n\nUse these categories in your fragment content:\n\n```markdown\n---\nbump: minor\n---\n\n### Added\n- Description of new feature\n\n### Changed\n- Description of change to existing functionality\n\n### Fixed\n- Description of bug fix\n\n### Removed\n- Description of removed feature\n\n### Deprecated\n- Description of deprecated feature\n\n### Security\n- Description of security fix\n```\n\n## Examples\n\n### Adding a new feature (minor bump)\n\n```markdown\n---\nbump: minor\n---\n\n### Added\n- New async processing mode for batch operations\n```\n\n### Fixing a bug (patch bump)\n\n```markdown\n---\nbump: patch\n---\n\n### Fixed\n- Fixed memory leak in connection pool handling\n```\n\n### Breaking change (major bump)\n\n```markdown\n---\nbump: major\n---\n\n### Changed\n- Renamed `process()` to `process_async()` - this is a breaking change\n\n### Removed\n- Removed deprecated `legacy_mode` option\n```\n\n## Why Fragments?\n\nUsing changelog fragments (similar to [Changesets](https://github.com/changesets/changesets) in JavaScript and [Scriv](https://scriv.readthedocs.io/) in Python):\n\n1. **No merge conflicts**: Multiple PRs can add fragments without conflicts\n2. **Per-PR documentation**: Each PR documents its own changes\n3. **Automated version bumping**: Version bump type is specified per-change\n4. **Automated collection**: Fragments are automatically collected during release\n5. **Consistent format**: Template ensures consistent changelog entries\n\n## How It Works\n\n1. **During PR**: Add a fragment file with your changes and bump type\n2. **On merge to main**: The release workflow automatically:\n   - Reads all fragment files and determines the highest bump type\n   - Bumps the version in `Cargo.toml` accordingly\n   - Collects fragments into `CHANGELOG.md`\n   - Creates a git tag and GitHub release\n   - Removes processed fragment files\n\n## Multiple PRs and Bump Priority\n\nWhen multiple PRs are merged before a release, all pending fragments are processed together. The **highest** bump type wins:\n\n- If any fragment specifies `major`, the release is a major version bump\n- Otherwise, if any specifies `minor`, the release is a minor version bump\n- Otherwise, the release is a patch version bump\n\nThis ensures that breaking changes are never missed, even when combined with smaller changes.\n\n## Default Behavior\n\nIf a fragment doesn't include a bump type in the frontmatter, it defaults to `patch`.\n=== check-file-size ===\n#!/usr/bin/env node\n\n/**\n * Check for files exceeding the maximum allowed line count\n * Exits with error code 1 if any files exceed the limit\n *\n * Uses link-foundation libraries:\n * - use-m: Dynamic package loading without package.json dependencies\n */\n\nimport { readFileSync, readdirSync, statSync } from 'fs';\nimport { join, relative, extname } from 'path';\n\nconst MAX_LINES = 1000;\nconst FILE_EXTENSIONS = ['.rs'];\nconst EXCLUDE_PATTERNS = ['target', '.git', 'node_modules'];\n\n/**\n * Check if a path should be excluded\n * @param {string} path\n * @returns {boolean}\n */\nfunction shouldExclude(path) {\n  return EXCLUDE_PATTERNS.some((pattern) => path.includes(pattern));\n}\n\n/**\n * Recursively find all Rust files in a directory\n * @param {string} directory\n * @returns {string[]}\n */\nfunction findRustFiles(directory) {\n  const files = [];\n\n  function walkDir(dir) {\n    const entries = readdirSync(dir, { withFileTypes: true });\n\n    for (const entry of entries) {\n      const fullPath = join(dir, entry.name);\n\n      if (shouldExclude(fullPath)) {\n        continue;\n      }\n\n      if (entry.isDirectory()) {\n        walkDir(fullPath);\n      } else if (entry.isFile() && FILE_EXTENSIONS.includes(extname(entry.name))) {\n        files.push(fullPath);\n      }\n    }\n  }\n\n  walkDir(directory);\n  return files;\n}\n\n/**\n * Count lines in a file\n * @param {string} filePath\n * @returns {number}",

changelog.d/README.md ---\n# Changelog Fragments\n\nThis directory contains changelog fragments that will be collected into `CHANGELOG.md` during releases.\n\n## How to Add a Changelog Fragment\n\nWhen making changes that should be documented in the changelog, create a fragment file:\n\n```bash\n# Create a new fragment with timestamp\ntouch changelog.d/$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S)_description.md\n\n# Or manually create a file matching the pattern: YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS_description.md\n```\n\n## Fragment Format\n\nEach fragment should include a **frontmatter section** specifying the version bump type:\n\n```markdown\n---\nbump: patch\n---\n\n### Fixed\n- Description of bug fix\n```\n\n### Bump Types\n\nUse semantic versioning bump types in the frontmatter:\n\n- **`major`**: Breaking changes (incompatible API changes)\n- **`minor`**: New features (backward compatible)\n- **`patch`**: Bug fixes (backward compatible)\n\n### Content Categories\n\nUse these categories in your fragment content:\n\n```markdown\n---\nbump: minor\n---\n\n### Added\n- Description of new feature\n\n### Changed\n- Description of change to existing functionality\n\n### Fixed\n- Description of bug fix\n\n### Removed\n- Description of removed feature\n\n### Deprecated\n- Description of deprecated feature\n\n### Security\n- Description of security fix\n```\n\n## Examples\n\n### Adding a new feature (minor bump)\n\n```markdown\n---\nbump: minor\n---\n\n### Added\n- New async processing mode for batch operations\n```\n\n### Fixing a bug (patch bump)\n\n```markdown\n---\nbump: patch\n---\n\n### Fixed\n- Fixed memory leak in connection pool handling\n```\n\n### Breaking change (major bump)\n\n```markdown\n---\nbump: major\n---\n\n### Changed\n- Renamed `process()` to `process_async()` - this is a breaking change\n\n### Removed\n- Removed deprecated `legacy_mode` option\n```\n\n## Why Fragments?\n\nUsing changelog fragments (similar to [Changesets](https://github.com/changesets/changesets) in JavaScript and [Scriv](https://scriv.readthedocs.io/) in Python):\n\n1. **No merge conflicts**: Multiple PRs can add fragments without conflicts\n2. **Per-PR documentation**: Each PR documents its own changes\n3. **Automated version bumping**: Version bump type is specified per-change\n4. **Automated collection**: Fragments are automatically collected during release\n5. **Consistent format**: Template ensures consistent changelog entries\n\n## How It Works\n\n1. **During PR**: Add a fragment file with your changes and bump type\n2. **On merge to main**: The release workflow automatically:\n   - Reads all fragment files and determines the highest bump type\n   - Bumps the version in `Cargo.toml` accordingly\n   - Collects fragments into `CHANGELOG.md`\n   - Creates a git tag and GitHub release\n   - Removes processed fragment files\n\n## Multiple PRs and Bump Priority\n\nWhen multiple PRs are merged before a release, all pending fragments are processed together. The **highest** bump type wins:\n\n- If any fragment specifies `major`, the release is a major version bump\n- Otherwise, if any specifies `minor`, the release is a minor version bump\n- Otherwise, the release is a patch version bump\n\nThis ensures that breaking changes are never missed, even when combined with smaller changes.\n\n## Default Behavior\n\nIf a fragment doesn't include a bump type in the frontmatter, it defaults to `patch`.\n=== check-file-size ===\n#!/usr/bin/env node\n\n/**\n * Check for files exceeding the maximum allowed line count\n * Exits with error code 1 if any files exceed the limit\n *\n * Uses link-foundation libraries:\n * - use-m: Dynamic package loading without package.json dependencies\n */\n\nimport { readFileSync, readdirSync, statSync } from 'fs';\nimport { join, relative, extname } from 'path';\n\nconst MAX_LINES = 1000;\nconst FILE_EXTENSIONS = ['.rs'];\nconst EXCLUDE_PATTERNS = ['target', '.git', 'node_modules'];\n\n/**\n * Check if a path should be excluded\n * @param {string} path\n * @returns {boolean}\n */\nfunction shouldExclude(path) {\n  return EXCLUDE_PATTERNS.some((pattern) => path.includes(pattern));\n}\n\n/**\n * Recursively find all Rust files in a directory\n * @param {string} directory\n * @returns {string[]}\n */\nfunction findRustFiles(directory) {\n  const files = [];\n\n  function walkDir(dir) {\n    const entries = readdirSync(dir, { withFileTypes: true });\n\n    for (const entry of entries) {\n      const fullPath = join(dir, entry.name);\n\n      if (shouldExclude(fullPath)) {\n        continue;\n      }\n\n      if (entry.isDirectory()) {\n        walkDir(fullPath);\n      } else if (entry.isFile() && FILE_EXTENSIONS.includes(extname(entry.name))) {\n        files.push(fullPath);\n      }\n    }\n  }\n\n  walkDir(directory);\n  return files;\n}\n\n/**\n * Count lines in a file\n * @param {string} filePath\n * @returns {number}",
